questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: Your company needs to deploy a web application that handles variable traffic. You want the application to scale automatically based on CPU utilization. Which Google Cloud service best fits this requirement?

options.A: Cloud Run with min-instances set to 0
options.B: Compute Engine managed instance group with autoscaling
options.C: Google Kubernetes Engine with manual node pools
options.D: Cloud Functions with a maximum concurrency of 1

questions.explanation: Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) support autoscaling based on CPU utilization, load balancing capacity, Cloud Monitoring metrics, or schedules. This is the canonical Compute Engine pattern for variable traffic. Cloud Run (A) also autoscales but is container-based, not VM-based. GKE with manual node pools (C) doesn't autoscale. Cloud Functions (D) is event-driven, not CPU-autoscaled.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: A developer needs read-only access to a specific Cloud Storage bucket. Following the principle of least privilege, what should you assign?

options.A: Project Viewer role at the project level
options.B: Storage Object Viewer role at the bucket level
options.C: Storage Admin role at the bucket level
options.D: Project Editor role at the organization level

questions.explanation: Least privilege means granting the minimum permission at the narrowest scope. roles/storage.objectViewer grants read-only access to objects within a bucket. Granting at bucket level (not project level) further limits blast radius. Project Viewer (A) grants read access to ALL project resources, not just one bucket.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to establish a private connection between your on-premises network and Google Cloud without traversing the public internet. Traffic volume is low and an SLA is not critical. Which option is most cost-effective?

options.A: Cloud Interconnect (Dedicated)
options.B: Cloud Interconnect (Partner)
options.C: Cloud VPN
options.D: Cloud CDN

questions.explanation: Cloud VPN creates an IPSec encrypted tunnel and is the most cost-effective option for low-volume traffic without SLA requirements. The traffic is private (encrypted tunnel) even though it travels over the internet infrastructure. Cloud Interconnect (A/B) provides physical private circuits with SLA guarantees but at significantly higher cost — justified only for high bandwidth or strict SLA needs. The keywords 'low volume' and 'SLA not critical' are the filters that point to VPN.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: Your application writes large streaming data (hundreds of GBs per day) and needs to query it with SQL. The data is only queried occasionally. Which storage solution is most appropriate?

options.A: Cloud SQL with PostgreSQL
options.B: Cloud Spanner
options.C: BigQuery
options.D: Bigtable

questions.explanation: BigQuery is a serverless OLAP data warehouse designed for large-scale analytics with SQL. It ingests streaming data via the Streaming API, stores it in columnar format, and you pay per query (bytes scanned) — ideal for occasional querying. Cloud Spanner (B) is OLTP: globally distributed, strongly consistent transactional database — expensive and wrong for analytics. Cloud SQL (A) cannot handle hundreds of GBs/day of streaming writes at low cost. The pattern 'streaming + SQL + occasional query + large volume' = BigQuery.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a stateless containerized microservice that needs to run only when triggered by HTTP requests. You want to minimize operational overhead and pay only for actual usage. What should you use?

options.A: Compute Engine n2-standard-2 instance
options.B: Cloud Run
options.C: GKE Autopilot cluster
options.D: App Engine Flexible environment

questions.explanation: Cloud Run is fully managed, scales to zero, and charges only for request processing time. It's the canonical GCP answer for stateless containerized HTTP workloads with zero ops overhead. GKE Autopilot (C) manages nodes but still has baseline cluster cost. App Engine Flex (D) doesn't scale to zero. Compute Engine (A) is always-on and requires full VM management.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: A team member left the company. You need to ensure they can no longer access Google Cloud resources while minimizing changes to project permissions. What is the recommended approach?

options.A: Delete all IAM policies from the project
options.B: Remove the user from the Google Workspace organization or suspend their Google account
options.C: Revoke only the Owner role from the user
options.D: Transfer all their resource ownership to another user

questions.explanation: Disabling or removing the identity at the IdP level (Google Workspace/Cloud Identity) immediately revokes all access across all GCP projects and resources in one action — no need to hunt down every IAM binding. This is the most comprehensive and operationally correct approach. Revoking only the Owner role (C) misses other roles they might have. Deleting all IAM policies (A) would break other users' access too.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: Your application is experiencing intermittent latency spikes. You want to identify the root cause by tracing requests across multiple microservices. Which GCP tool should you use?

options.A: Cloud Logging
options.B: Cloud Monitoring dashboards
options.C: Cloud Trace
options.D: Cloud Profiler

questions.explanation: Cloud Trace is the distributed tracing service — it records latency data for requests as they propagate across microservices, showing you exactly where time is spent in each span. Cloud Logging (A) records events but doesn't show request flow across services. Cloud Profiler (D) continuously profiles CPU/memory consumption of running code, useful for performance optimization but not cross-service tracing. Cloud Monitoring (B) shows metrics but not trace-level request paths.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You need a database for a global e-commerce application that requires strong consistency, relational structure, and horizontal scalability across multiple regions. Which service should you choose?

options.A: Cloud SQL (MySQL) with read replicas
options.B: Cloud Spanner
options.C: Firestore in Datastore mode
options.D: Bigtable

questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner is the only relational database that is horizontally scalable and globally distributed with external strong consistency. Cloud SQL (A) is single-region primary with read replicas — no true horizontal write scaling. Firestore (C) is NoSQL document store — not relational. Bigtable (D) is a wide-column NoSQL store for time-series and analytical workloads. 'Relational + strong consistency + global + horizontal scale' is Spanner's exact value proposition.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to restrict outbound internet access from VM instances in a VPC but allow them to access Google APIs and services. What should you configure?

options.A: A Cloud NAT gateway and Private Google Access
options.B: A Cloud Load Balancer with SSL policy
options.C: VPC Service Controls with an access policy
options.D: A Cloud Armor security policy

questions.explanation: Cloud NAT provides outbound internet access for VMs without external IPs (you can restrict this per subnet). Private Google Access allows VMs without external IPs to reach Google APIs (storage.googleapis.com, etc.) through Google's internal network without traversing the internet. These two features together give you controlled, private-by-default egress. VPC Service Controls (C) restrict API access by perimeter, not egress routing. Cloud Armor (D) is a WAF/DDoS shield for inbound traffic.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to deploy a new version of your App Engine application without any downtime. After deployment, you want to route 10% of traffic to the new version to test it. What should you use?

options.A: Rolling updates with App Engine Flexible
options.B: Traffic splitting in App Engine
options.C: Blue-green deployment with Cloud Load Balancing
options.D: Canary deployment using Cloud Run revisions

questions.explanation: App Engine has built-in traffic splitting as a native feature. You deploy a new version and use `gcloud app services set-traffic [SERVICE] --splits v1=0.9,v2=0.1` to route percentages. No external load balancer needed. Blue-green with Cloud Load Balancing (C) is valid for GKE/Compute Engine but requires extra infrastructure for App Engine. Cloud Run revisions (D) is correct for Cloud Run, not App Engine. The exam tests whether you know which platform has which native capability.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are running a batch job that processes data once a day at midnight and takes approximately 2 hours to complete. The job is fault-tolerant and can be restarted. Which Compute Engine option minimizes cost?

options.A: n2-standard-4 on-demand instances
options.B: n2-standard-4 reserved instances (1-year commitment)
options.C: Spot VMs (formerly preemptible VMs)
options.D: Sole-tenant nodes

questions.explanation: Spot VMs (formerly preemptible) offer up to 91% discount over on-demand prices. The two required conditions are both met: (1) fault-tolerant — the job can be restarted if preempted, and (2) the runtime is under 24 hours. For a 2-hour batch job at midnight, even if preempted once, it can restart and complete before morning. Committed use (B) makes sense for always-on workloads. Sole-tenant nodes (D) are for licensing/compliance isolation and cost more.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your organization wants to ensure that no Cloud Storage buckets in any project can be made publicly accessible. How do you enforce this at scale?

options.A: Set the Public access prevention policy on every bucket individually
options.B: Apply an Organization Policy constraint that prevents public IAM grants on Cloud Storage
options.C: Enable VPC Service Controls on all projects
options.D: Create a Cloud Armor policy blocking anonymous access

questions.explanation: Organization Policies are the correct mechanism for enforcing guardrails at scale across all projects. The constraint `storage.publicAccessPrevention` when set to `enforced` at the org level prevents allUsers and allAuthenticatedUsers from being granted on any bucket in any project. Setting it bucket-by-bucket (A) doesn't scale and misses new buckets. VPC Service Controls (C) restrict API access by network perimeter, not public IAM. Cloud Armor (D) is a network-layer control, not an IAM control.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to expose an internal microservice running in GKE to other services within the same cluster, but not to the internet. Which Kubernetes resource type should you use?

options.A: LoadBalancer Service
options.B: Ingress with a global external HTTP(S) load balancer
options.C: ClusterIP Service
options.D: NodePort Service

questions.explanation: ClusterIP is the default Service type. It assigns a virtual IP reachable only from within the cluster — perfect for service-to-service communication. LoadBalancer (A) provisions a Google Cloud external LB with a public IP. Ingress (B) routes external HTTP/HTTPS traffic to backend services via an external LB. NodePort (D) exposes a port on every node, accessible from outside the cluster if the node IP is reachable. For internal-only cluster communication: always ClusterIP.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: Your application stores user profile images uploaded by clients. Images are accessed frequently for the first 30 days, then rarely accessed. You want to minimize storage costs while maintaining availability. What should you configure?

options.A: Store all images in Cloud Storage Nearline class
options.B: Store all images in Cloud Storage Standard class
options.C: Use Object Lifecycle Management to transition objects to Coldline after 30 days
options.D: Use Object Lifecycle Management to transition objects to Nearline after 30 days

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage classes by access frequency: Standard (frequent), Nearline (less than once/month, 30-day minimum storage), Coldline (less than once/quarter, 90-day minimum), Archive (less than once/year, 365-day minimum). 'Rarely accessed after 30 days' maps to Nearline — not Coldline. Coldline would be excessive and has higher retrieval costs if the images are accessed at all. Object Lifecycle Management automates the transition without manual intervention. Start in Standard, transition to Nearline at day 30.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to be alerted when the error rate of your Cloud Run service exceeds 5% over a 5-minute window. What is the correct approach?

options.A: Create a log-based metric from Cloud Logging, then create an alerting policy on that metric
options.B: Set up a Cloud Scheduler job that queries logs every 5 minutes
options.C: Enable Cloud Trace and set a threshold on trace latency
options.D: Use Cloud Profiler to detect error rate anomalies

questions.explanation: Log-based metrics convert log entries into numeric Cloud Monitoring metrics. You create a counter metric filtering for HTTP 5xx status codes, then create an alerting policy that fires when error_rate > 5% over a 5-minute window. This is the standard GCP pattern for log-derived alerting. Cloud Scheduler (B) is a cron service, not a monitoring tool. Cloud Trace (C) measures latency, not error rates. Cloud Profiler (D) profiles code performance.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: A legacy application requires a specific version of an operating system and must run on dedicated physical hardware for licensing compliance. Which Compute Engine feature fulfills this requirement?

options.A: Custom machine types
options.B: Spot VMs
options.C: Sole-tenant nodes
options.D: Confidential VMs

questions.explanation: Sole-tenant nodes are physical Compute Engine servers dedicated exclusively to your project — no other customer's VMs share the host. This satisfies: (1) software licenses tied to physical sockets/cores (BYOL), and (2) compliance requirements prohibiting multi-tenancy. Custom machine types (A) let you pick CPU/RAM ratios but run on shared hardware. Spot VMs (B) are preemptible discount instances — opposite of dedicated. Confidential VMs (D) encrypt data in-use via AMD SEV, addressing data confidentiality, not hardware dedication.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You use Cloud Build to build container images. You want to ensure that only images built by your approved Cloud Build pipeline can be deployed to your GKE cluster. Which service enforces this?

options.A: Binary Authorization
options.B: Cloud Armor
options.C: VPC Service Controls
options.D: Container Analysis

questions.explanation: Binary Authorization is a deploy-time security control for GKE. It requires that container images have a cryptographic attestation (signed by a trusted authority) before they can be deployed. You configure Cloud Build to attest images after a successful build. The GKE admission controller then rejects any image without the required attestation. Cloud Armor (B) is a WAF/DDoS protection for inbound network traffic — unrelated to image provenance. Container Analysis (D) scans for vulnerabilities but doesn't enforce deployment policy. VPC Service Controls (C) restrict API access by network perimeter.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You have multiple GCP projects and want to share a single VPC network across all of them to simplify network management and reduce IP address consumption. What should you implement?

options.A: VPC Peering between all projects
options.B: Shared VPC with a host project
options.C: Cloud Interconnect with a transit VPC
options.D: Multiple independent VPCs with Private Service Connect

questions.explanation: Shared VPC lets an organization designate a host project whose VPC subnets are shared with service projects. All VMs in service projects use the host project's network, giving you centralized network management, unified firewall rules, and efficient IP usage — no duplication. VPC Peering (A) connects independent VPCs but each project still manages its own network, and peering is not transitive. Cloud Interconnect (C) is for on-premises connectivity. Private Service Connect (D) is for accessing managed services privately.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to store session state for a web application with sub-millisecond read latency and the ability to set TTL on entries automatically. Which managed service fits best?

options.A: Cloud SQL (PostgreSQL)
options.B: Firestore
options.C: Memorystore for Redis
options.D: Cloud Bigtable

questions.explanation: Memorystore for Redis is a fully managed in-memory data store. Redis operates entirely in RAM, delivering sub-millisecond latency. Native TTL support (`SET key value EX seconds`) automatically expires session entries. This is the industry standard for session caching. Firestore (B) is a NoSQL document database with millisecond-range latency — not sub-millisecond. Cloud SQL (A) is a disk-based relational DB, latency in tens of milliseconds. Bigtable (D) is for high-throughput analytical/time-series workloads. The keyword 'sub-millisecond' always points to an in-memory store.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: An auditor needs to review all administrative actions performed on GCP resources over the past 90 days across your organization. Where do you find this information?

options.A: Cloud Monitoring metrics explorer
options.B: Cloud Audit Logs — Admin Activity logs
options.C: VPC Flow Logs
options.D: Cloud Trace timeline

questions.explanation: Cloud Audit Logs records who did what, where, and when on GCP resources. Admin Activity logs capture administrative actions (creating, modifying, deleting resources, changing IAM policies) and are always on, retained for 400 days. They are the authoritative source for compliance audits. VPC Flow Logs (C) record network traffic metadata. Cloud Monitoring (A) tracks metrics. Cloud Trace (D) records request latency.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: Your GKE nodes are consistently running at 85% CPU. New Pods are failing to schedule. What is the correct action to resolve this without changing the number of nodes manually?

options.A: Enable the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) on the Deployment
options.B: Enable the Cluster Autoscaler on the node pool
options.C: Increase the CPU request limit in the Pod spec
options.D: Add a node affinity rule to spread Pods evenly

questions.explanation: The Cluster Autoscaler automatically adds nodes when Pods are in Pending state due to insufficient resources, and removes nodes when they are underutilized. The problem is at the node layer (not enough node capacity), so the fix is at the node layer. HPA (A) adds more Pod replicas — but if nodes are already full, more Pods will also be Pending, making the problem worse. Increasing CPU requests (C) makes each Pod demand more resources, further reducing schedulability. Node affinity rules (D) change Pod placement preferences but don't create capacity.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to migrate an on-premises MySQL database to Google Cloud with minimal downtime and zero data loss during cutover. Which approach does Google recommend?

options.A: Export a mysqldump file, copy to Cloud Storage, import into Cloud SQL
options.B: Use Database Migration Service with continuous replication
options.C: Create a Cloud SQL instance and restore from a nightly backup
options.D: Use Dataflow to stream CDC events from on-premises MySQL to Cloud SQL

questions.explanation: Database Migration Service (DMS) is Google's managed migration service for MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server. It establishes continuous replication via binlog, so the Cloud SQL instance stays in sync with on-premises until you're ready to cut over — achieving minimal downtime and zero data loss. mysqldump (A) requires downtime during the export window and causes data loss for transactions during migration. A nightly backup (C) guarantees data loss of up to 24 hours.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to centralize logs from all projects in your GCP organization into a single Cloud Storage bucket for long-term retention and compliance. What is the correct configuration?

options.A: Create a log sink at the project level in each project
options.B: Create an aggregated log sink at the organization level
options.C: Enable Cloud Monitoring metrics export for all projects
options.D: Use Pub/Sub to forward logs from each project to a central topic

questions.explanation: Aggregated log sinks (also called organization-level sinks) capture logs from all projects, folders, or the entire organization in a single configuration. You create one sink at the organization level with destination set to a Cloud Storage bucket, and it automatically includes logs from all child resources. Project-level sinks (A) require one configuration per project and miss new projects. Pub/Sub aggregation (D) would work but requires additional infrastructure to read from Pub/Sub and write to Storage. Cloud Monitoring metrics export (C) exports metrics, not logs.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You are creating a service account for a Cloud Run service that needs to read from a specific BigQuery dataset. Following least privilege, what role should you grant?

options.A: roles/bigquery.admin on the project
options.B: roles/bigquery.dataViewer on the specific dataset
options.C: roles/bigquery.user on the project
options.D: roles/viewer on the project

questions.explanation: roles/bigquery.dataViewer grants read access (list and get tables, read data) on the resource it's applied to. Granting it at the dataset level (not project level) limits the scope to only that dataset. roles/bigquery.admin (A) grants full control over all BigQuery resources. roles/bigquery.user (C) allows running jobs and accessing datasets the user has been explicitly granted access to — still project-scoped. roles/viewer (D) is a basic role that grants read access to ALL project resources.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: Users report that your HTTPS website is slow for international visitors. Your origin is in us-central1. You want to improve global performance with minimal infrastructure changes. What should you enable?

options.A: Cloud Interconnect to additional regions
options.B: Cloud CDN on your existing external HTTPS load balancer
options.C: Cloud DNS with latency-based routing policies
options.D: Premium network tier for your VMs

questions.explanation: Cloud CDN caches responses at Google's global edge PoPs (Points of Presence) closest to users. International visitors hit the nearest edge node instead of the origin in us-central1, dramatically reducing latency. It enables with a single checkbox on an existing Cloud Load Balancing backend service — minimal infrastructure change. Cloud Interconnect (A) is for on-premises connectivity. Cloud DNS latency routing (C) routes DNS queries to the nearest regional endpoint but doesn't cache content. Premium network tier (D) uses Google's backbone for egress but doesn't cache at the edge.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to run a containerized job that processes a file and exits when done. The job must run to completion and you want Kubernetes to manage retries on failure. What resource should you create in GKE?

options.A: Deployment with restartPolicy: Always
options.B: DaemonSet
options.C: StatefulSet
options.D: Job with restartPolicy: OnFailure

questions.explanation: A Kubernetes Job creates one or more Pods and ensures a specified number of them complete successfully. restartPolicy: OnFailure restarts the container within the same Pod on failure (or creates a new Pod if the node fails). This is exactly the pattern for run-to-completion batch workloads. Deployment (A) with Always restart policy keeps Pods running indefinitely — for long-running services, not batch jobs. DaemonSet (B) runs one Pod per node. StatefulSet (C) manages stateful applications with stable network identities and persistent storage.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: Your application needs to publish events when a new object is uploaded to a Cloud Storage bucket, so downstream services can react in real time. What is the recommended pattern?

options.A: Poll the bucket every minute using a Cloud Scheduler job
options.B: Enable Pub/Sub notifications on the Cloud Storage bucket
options.C: Use Cloud Functions HTTP trigger to monitor bucket changes
options.D: Create a Cloud Logging sink to export object creation events to Pub/Sub

questions.explanation: Cloud Storage has native Pub/Sub notifications. When enabled, GCS publishes a message to a Pub/Sub topic for each object event (OBJECT_FINALIZE, OBJECT_DELETE, etc.). Downstream services subscribe to the topic and react in near-real time. This is event-driven and requires no polling or additional infrastructure. Polling with Cloud Scheduler (A) adds latency and wastes resources. Cloud Functions can trigger on GCS events directly (which internally uses Pub/Sub), but option B is the more primitive/correct building block. Logging sink (D) adds unnecessary latency through the logging pipeline.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your organization has a requirement that all resources must reside in specific geographic regions for data residency compliance. How do you enforce this at the organization level?

options.A: Tag all resources with a 'region' label and audit periodically
options.B: Apply an Organization Policy with the gcp.resourceLocations constraint
options.C: Configure VPC Service Controls to restrict regions
options.D: Use Cloud Asset Inventory with a custom alert

questions.explanation: The Organization Policy constraint gcp.resourceLocations allows you to restrict the set of locations where Google Cloud resources can be created. Setting it at the org level means no resource in any child project or folder can be deployed outside the allowed regions — enforcement is proactive, not reactive. Labels + auditing (A) is detective control, not preventive — resources can still be created in wrong regions. VPC Service Controls (C) control network-level API access perimeters, not resource location. Cloud Asset Inventory + alerts (D) is also detective, not preventive.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to SSH into a Compute Engine VM that has no external IP address and no bastion host. The VM has the necessary OS Login configured. What is the simplest approach?

options.A: Assign a temporary external IP to the VM
options.B: Use Cloud Shell with gcloud compute ssh via IAP (Identity-Aware Proxy)
options.C: Set up a Cloud VPN to access the private IP directly
options.D: Use a NAT gateway to route SSH traffic

questions.explanation: IAP TCP forwarding allows you to tunnel SSH connections through Google's infrastructure to VMs without external IPs and without a bastion host. The command `gcloud compute ssh INSTANCE --tunnel-through-iap` establishes a secure tunnel authenticated via IAP. No external IP, no VPN, no bastion needed. IAP checks the user's IAM permissions before allowing the tunnel. This is the GCP-native zero-trust SSH access pattern. Adding a temporary external IP (A) is a security anti-pattern. VPN (C) requires significant setup for a simple SSH need.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to define an SLO for your API: 99.5% of requests must complete in under 300ms over a 30-day rolling window. In Cloud Monitoring SLO framework, what type of SLI should you configure?

options.A: Availability SLI based on uptime checks
options.B: Latency SLI using a request-based good/bad event ratio
options.C: Error rate SLI based on HTTP 5xx responses
options.D: Throughput SLI measuring requests per second

questions.explanation: A request-based Latency SLI classifies each request as 'good' (completed in < 300ms) or 'bad' (exceeded threshold). The SLO then states that 99.5% of all requests must be good over the window. This is the correct SLI type for latency-based SLOs in Cloud Monitoring. Availability SLI (A) measures uptime/downtime, not latency. Error rate SLI (C) counts HTTP 5xx responses — for reliability, not speed. Throughput SLI (D) measures volume (requests/second), not whether individual requests were fast. The structure 'X% of requests must complete in under Yms' = request-based Latency SLI.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You significantly changed a Deployment Manager template and want to confirm that the dependencies of all defined resources are properly met before committing it to the project. You want the most rapid feedback. What should you do?

options.A: Use granular logging statements within a Deployment Manager template authored in Python
options.B: Monitor activity of the Deployment Manager execution on the Cloud Logging page
options.C: Execute the Deployment Manager template against a separate project with the same configuration
options.D: Execute the Deployment Manager template using the --preview option in the same project

questions.explanation: The --preview flag validates the template and shows what changes would be made without actually applying them. It's the fastest way to check for dependency errors. Running against a separate project (C) wastes time and resources. Logging (A, B) only provides feedback after execution has started.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a 3-tier application on Compute Engine. Each tier has a service account associated with all instances. You need to enable communication on TCP port 8080: tier 1 to tier 2, and tier 2 to tier 3. What should you do?

options.A: Create ingress firewall rules with IP range source filters for each tier's subnet
options.B: Create ingress firewall rules using service accounts as source and target filters on TCP 8080
options.C: Create ingress firewall rules using service accounts as source and target filters allowing all protocols
options.D: Create egress firewall rules using IP range filters for each tier's subnet on TCP 8080

questions.explanation: Using service accounts as firewall rule source/target is the Google-recommended practice for inter-service communication. It's more precise than IP ranges (which change as instances scale) and allows specifying only TCP 8080. Option C allows all protocols unnecessarily. Egress rules (D) would be the wrong direction — you need ingress rules on the receiving tier.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You are analyzing GCP service costs from three separate projects. You want to create service cost estimates by service type, daily and monthly, for the next six months using standard SQL. What should you do?

options.A: Export your bill to a Cloud Storage bucket, then import into Cloud Bigtable for analysis
options.B: Export your bill to a Cloud Storage bucket, then import into Google Sheets for analysis
options.C: Export your transactions to a local file and perform analysis with a desktop tool
options.D: Export your bill to a BigQuery dataset, and then write time window-based SQL queries for analysis

questions.explanation: BigQuery Billing Export is Google's recommended approach for billing analysis. You enable export to BigQuery from the Billing console, and then use standard SQL to query costs by service type, date, and project. This supports time window queries, daily/monthly aggregations, and future estimates using SQL — something Sheets (B) or Bigtable (A) cannot do natively with SQL.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You want to send and consume Cloud Pub/Sub messages from your App Engine application. The Cloud Pub/Sub API is currently disabled. You will use a service account to authenticate. What should you do first?

options.A: Enable the Cloud Pub/Sub API in the API Library on the GCP Console
options.B: Rely on the automatic enablement of the Cloud Pub/Sub API when the Service Account accesses it
options.C: Use Deployment Manager to deploy your application and rely on automatic API enablement
options.D: Grant the App Engine Default service account the role of Cloud Pub/Sub Admin

questions.explanation: APIs must be explicitly enabled before they can be used — they do not auto-enable when accessed. You enable APIs via the API Library in the Cloud Console or via gcloud. Granting IAM roles (D) does not enable the API itself; both steps are required, but enabling the API comes first.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a website hosted on App Engine standard environment. You want 1% of your users to see a new test version with minimum complexity. What should you do?

options.A: Deploy the new version in the same application and use the --migrate option
options.B: Deploy the new version in the same application and use the --splits option to give a weight of 99 to current and 1 to new
options.C: Create a new App Engine application in the same project and use the App Engine library to proxy 1% of requests
options.D: Create a new App Engine application in the same project and configure your network load balancer to send 1% traffic

questions.explanation: App Engine's built-in traffic splitting with the --splits option is the simplest way to route a percentage of traffic to a new version. You don't need a new application or external load balancer. The --migrate option (A) gradually moves all traffic to the new version, not 1%.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: Your organization is a financial company that needs to store audit log files for 3 years across hundreds of GCP projects. You need a cost-effective approach for retention. What should you do?

options.A: Create a log sink that exports Cloud Audit logs to BigQuery
options.B: Create a log sink that exports Cloud Audit logs to a Coldline Storage bucket
options.C: Write a custom script using the logging API to copy logs from Cloud Logging to BigQuery
options.D: Export logs to Cloud Pub/Sub and write a Dataflow pipeline to store logs in Cloud SQL

questions.explanation: For long-term retention of audit logs (3 years), Coldline Storage is the most cost-effective option — it's designed for infrequently accessed data with low per-GB storage cost. A log sink to Coldline requires no custom code. BigQuery (A) is more expensive for pure storage of rarely queried historical logs. Custom scripts (C, D) add unnecessary operational complexity.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You built an application on Google Cloud that uses Cloud Spanner. Your support team needs to monitor the environment but should not have access to table data. What should you do?

options.A: Add the support team group to the roles/monitoring.viewer role
options.B: Add the support team group to the roles/spanner.databaseUser role
options.C: Add the support team group to the roles/spanner.databaseReader role
options.D: Add the support team group to the roles/stackdriver.accounts.viewer role

questions.explanation: roles/monitoring.viewer grants read-only access to Cloud Monitoring metrics and dashboards without any access to the actual data in Spanner tables. roles/spanner.databaseUser (B) and databaseReader (C) both grant access to read table data, which is explicitly prohibited. For monitoring without data access, the Monitoring Viewer role is the correct choice.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to run a single caching HTTP reverse proxy on GCP for a latency-sensitive website. This proxy needs a 30-GB in-memory cache and an additional 2 GB for other processes. You want to minimize cost. How should you run this?

options.A: Create a Cloud Memorystore for Redis instance with 32-GB capacity
options.B: Run it on Compute Engine with a custom instance type with 6 vCPUs and 32 GB of memory
options.C: Package it in a container and run it on GKE using n1-standard-32 nodes
options.D: Run it on Compute Engine with n1-standard-1 and add an SSD persistent disk of 32 GB

questions.explanation: Cloud Memorystore for Redis is a fully managed in-memory caching service. For a caching proxy that needs 32 GB in-memory and consumes almost no CPU, Memorystore provides a cost-effective managed solution without needing to provision or manage Compute Engine instances. Running a Compute Engine custom instance (B) or GKE (C) adds unnecessary infrastructure cost and management overhead.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a single binary application you want to run on GCP. You need to automatically scale based on underlying infrastructure CPU usage. Your organizational policies require virtual machines directly. What should you do?

options.A: Create a GKE cluster and use horizontal pod autoscaling
options.B: Create an instance template and use it in a Managed Instance Group with autoscaling configured
options.C: Create an instance template and use it in a Managed Instance Group that scales based on time of day
options.D: Use third-party tools to build automation around scaling based on Cloud Monitoring CPU metrics

questions.explanation: A Managed Instance Group with autoscaling is the standard GCP approach for automatically scaling VM-based applications. The policy requires VMs directly (not containers/GKE), so option A is invalid. Time-based scaling (C) doesn't respond dynamically to actual CPU load. Third-party tools (D) add unnecessary complexity when GCP provides this natively.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You need to set up permissions for Compute Engine instances to write data into a specific Cloud Storage bucket. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Create a service account with access scope https://www.googleapis.com/auth/devstorage.write_only
options.B: Create a service account with access scope https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform
options.C: Create a service account and add it to the IAM role storage.objectCreator for that bucket
options.D: Create a service account and add it to the IAM role storage.objectAdmin for that bucket

questions.explanation: Google's recommended practice is to use IAM roles (not access scopes) to grant permissions. roles/storage.objectCreator grants only the ability to create objects, which is the minimum necessary for writing data. objectAdmin (D) also grants delete and update permissions, violating least privilege. Access scopes (A, B) are an older mechanism that should not be relied upon alone.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You have an object in a Cloud Storage bucket you want to share with an external company for four hours. The company does not have a Google account. You want to use the most secure method that requires the fewest steps. What should you do?

options.A: Create a signed URL with a four-hour expiration and share the URL with the company
options.B: Set object access to 'public' and use object lifecycle management to remove it after four hours
options.C: Configure the bucket as a static website and furnish the URL, then delete after four hours
options.D: Create a new bucket specifically for the company, copy the object, and delete the bucket after four hours

questions.explanation: Signed URLs provide time-limited access to Cloud Storage objects without requiring a Google account. They use cryptographic signatures and automatically expire after the specified duration — four hours in this case. Making the object public (B) gives access to everyone on the internet, not just the intended company. Options C and D require more steps.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to create an autoscaling Managed Instance Group for an HTTPS web application. You want to make sure that unhealthy VMs are recreated. What should you do?

options.A: Create a health check on port 443 and use that when creating the Managed Instance Group
options.B: Select Multi-Zone instead of Single-Zone when creating the Managed Instance Group
options.C: In the Instance Template, add the label 'health-check'
options.D: In the Instance Template, add a startup script that sends a heartbeat to the metadata server

questions.explanation: MIGs support autohealing using health checks. When you attach a health check to a MIG, it monitors the HTTP(S) status of instances and automatically recreates any VM that fails. A health check on port 443 is appropriate for HTTPS applications. Labels (C) and startup scripts (D) have no built-in autohealing effect.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are deploying an application to a Compute Engine VM in a Managed Instance Group. The application must run at all times, but only a single instance should run per GCP project. How should you configure the instance group?

options.A: Set autoscaling to On, min instances to 1, max instances to 1
options.B: Set autoscaling to Off, min instances to 1, max instances to 1
options.C: Set autoscaling to On, min instances to 1, max instances to 2
options.D: Set autoscaling to Off, min instances to 1, max instances to 2

questions.explanation: Setting autoscaling to On with min=1 and max=1 ensures exactly one instance runs at all times. The MIG will recreate the instance if it fails (autohealing). Setting autoscaling to Off (B) still maintains the instance count but loses the autoscaling health management. Max of 2 (C, D) could allow a second instance to be created.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You have production and test workloads that you want to deploy on Compute Engine. Production VMs need to be in a different subnet than test VMs. All VMs must reach each other over internal IP without additional routes. What VPC configuration meets these requirements?

options.A: Create a single custom VPC with 2 subnets in different regions with different CIDR ranges
options.B: Create a single custom VPC with 2 subnets in the same region with the same CIDR range
options.C: Create 2 custom VPCs each with a single subnet in different regions with different CIDR ranges
options.D: Create 2 custom VPCs each with a single subnet in the same region with the same CIDR range

questions.explanation: A single VPC with multiple subnets allows all instances to communicate via internal IP without additional routes — VPCs provide a flat network space across all subnets. Different subnets with different CIDR ranges in different regions satisfy the isolation requirement. Two separate VPCs (C, D) would require VPC peering for internal communication.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have an instance group that you want to load balance. You want the load balancer to terminate the client SSL session. The instance group serves a public web application over HTTPS. What should you do?

options.A: Configure an HTTP(S) load balancer
options.B: Configure an internal TCP load balancer
options.C: Configure an external SSL proxy load balancer
options.D: Configure an external TCP proxy load balancer

questions.explanation: An HTTP(S) load balancer (Cloud Load Balancing) is a Layer 7 global load balancer that terminates SSL connections at the load balancer level and routes traffic based on HTTP content. It's the recommended option for public HTTPS web applications. An internal TCP LB (B) doesn't serve public traffic. SSL proxy (C) is for non-HTTP SSL traffic.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a web application deployed as a Managed Instance Group. You have a new version to gradually deploy. Your web application is currently receiving live web traffic. You want to ensure that the available capacity does not decrease during deployment. What should you do?

options.A: Perform a rolling-action start-update with maxSurge set to 0 and maxUnavailable set to 1
options.B: Perform a rolling-action start-update with maxSurge set to 1 and maxUnavailable set to 0
options.C: Create a new MIG with an updated instance template, add to the backend service, then delete the old MIG
options.D: Create a new instance template, update the existing MIG, then delete old instances to allow recreation

questions.explanation: maxSurge=1 allows one additional instance above the target size during update (temporarily increasing capacity), and maxUnavailable=0 ensures no instances are taken offline during the update. This guarantees capacity is never reduced. maxSurge=0 and maxUnavailable=1 (A) means one instance is taken down before a new one comes up — reducing capacity.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You need to grant access for three users so that they can view and edit table data on a Cloud Spanner instance. Following Google-recommended practices, what should you do?

options.A: Run gcloud iam roles describe roles/spanner.databaseUser. Add the users to the role.
options.B: Run gcloud iam roles describe roles/spanner.databaseUser. Add users to a new group. Add the group to the role.
options.C: Run gcloud iam roles describe roles/spanner.viewer. Add the users to the role.
options.D: Run gcloud iam roles describe roles/spanner.viewer. Add users to a group. Add the group to the role.

questions.explanation: roles/spanner.databaseUser grants read and write access to Spanner database tables. Google recommends managing access via groups rather than individual users — adding users to a group and then assigning the role to the group makes future access management easier. roles/spanner.viewer (C, D) is read-only and doesn't meet the 'view and edit' requirement.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You need to create a new billing account and then link it with an existing Google Cloud Platform project. What should you do?

options.A: Verify that you are Project Billing Manager for the GCP project. Update the existing project to link it to the existing billing account.
options.B: Verify that you are Project Billing Manager for the GCP project. Create a new billing account and link it to the existing project.
options.C: Verify that you are Billing Administrator for the billing account. Create a new project and link it to the existing billing account.
options.D: Verify that you are Billing Administrator for the billing account. Update the existing project to link it to the existing billing account.

questions.explanation: To create a new billing account you need Billing Administrator rights. To link a billing account to a project you need Project Billing Manager rights. Option B correctly identifies that the user needs Project Billing Manager rights to link the new billing account to the existing project. Option D confuses the roles — Billing Admin alone doesn't let you link projects.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You have sensitive data stored in three Cloud Storage buckets with data access logging enabled. You want to verify activities for a particular user for these buckets using the fewest possible steps — specifically addition of metadata labels and which files have been viewed. What should you do?

options.A: Using the GCP Console, filter the Activity log to view the information
options.B: Using the GCP Console, filter the Stackdriver log to view the information
options.C: View the bucket in the Storage section of the GCP Console
options.D: Create a trace in Cloud Trace to view the information

questions.explanation: The Cloud Console Activity log (now Cloud Audit Logs) shows both Admin Activity (metadata changes like label additions) and Data Access logs (file views). Filtering by the specific user's email gives you a consolidated view of their activities across buckets. The Storage section (C) shows bucket content, not access logs. Cloud Trace (D) is for latency tracing.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to run an important query in BigQuery but expect it to return a lot of records. You want to find out how much it will cost to run the query. You are using on-demand pricing. What should you do?

options.A: Arrange to switch to Flat-Rate pricing for this query, then move back to on-demand
options.B: Use the command line to run a dry run query to estimate the number of bytes read. Then convert using the Pricing Calculator.
options.C: Use the command line to run a dry run query to estimate the number of bytes returned. Then convert using the Pricing Calculator.
options.D: Run a select count(*) to get an idea of how many records your query will look through

questions.explanation: BigQuery on-demand pricing is based on bytes read (scanned), not bytes returned. The bq --dry_run flag runs the query without executing it and reports the estimated bytes that will be processed. You then multiply that by the per-TB price to estimate cost. Switching to flat-rate (A) is expensive and unnecessary for a one-time estimate.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to monitor resources distributed over different projects in Google Cloud Platform. You want to consolidate reporting under the same Cloud Monitoring dashboard. What should you do?

options.A: Use Shared VPC to connect all projects, and link Cloud Monitoring to one of the projects
options.B: For each project, create a Cloud Monitoring workspace. Create service accounts with cross-project Monitoring Editor access.
options.C: Configure a single Cloud Monitoring workspace, and link all projects to the same workspace
options.D: Configure a Cloud Monitoring workspace for one project. Create a Group and add the other project names as criteria.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring supports multi-project workspaces where a single workspace can monitor resources across multiple GCP projects. You create a workspace in one project and then add the other projects as monitored projects. All metrics and dashboards are consolidated in the single workspace view.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need a dynamic way of provisioning VMs on Compute Engine. The exact specifications will be in a dedicated configuration file. You want to follow Google's recommended practices. Which method should you use?

options.A: Deployment Manager
options.B: Cloud Composer
options.C: Managed Instance Group
options.D: Unmanaged Instance Group

questions.explanation: Deployment Manager is Google Cloud's Infrastructure as Code (IaC) service. It allows you to describe your infrastructure in YAML or Python configuration files and deploy it reproducibly. Cloud Composer (B) is a managed Apache Airflow for workflow orchestration. MIGs (C) manage instance groups but don't use external configuration files for arbitrary resource specs.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are creating a GKE cluster and want to make sure that each node of the cluster will run a monitoring pod that sends container metrics to a third-party monitoring solution. What should you do?

options.A: Deploy the monitoring pod in a StatefulSet object
options.B: Deploy the monitoring pod in a DaemonSet object
options.C: Reference the monitoring pod in a Deployment object
options.D: Reference the monitoring pod in a cluster initializer at GKE cluster creation time

questions.explanation: A DaemonSet ensures that a copy of a Pod runs on all (or selected) nodes in the cluster. As new nodes are added to the cluster, Pods are added to them automatically. This is the standard pattern for deploying cluster-wide infrastructure agents like monitoring, logging, or security tools.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You create a new GKE cluster and want to make sure that it always runs a supported and stable version of Kubernetes. What should you do?

options.A: Enable the Node Auto-Repair feature for your GKE cluster
options.B: Enable the Node Auto-Upgrades feature for your GKE cluster
options.C: Select the latest available cluster version for your GKE cluster
options.D: Select Container-Optimized OS (cos) as a node image for your GKE cluster

questions.explanation: Node Auto-Upgrades automatically keeps your cluster nodes updated with the latest stable version of the Kubernetes node software. This ensures your cluster stays on supported versions as new versions are released. Node Auto-Repair (A) automatically repairs unhealthy nodes but doesn't upgrade the Kubernetes version.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: Your company uses Cloud Storage to store application backup files for disaster recovery purposes. You want to follow Google's recommended practices. Which storage option should you use?

options.A: Multi-Regional Storage
options.B: Regional Storage
options.C: Nearline Storage
options.D: Coldline Storage

questions.explanation: Backup files for disaster recovery are infrequently accessed — typically accessed less than once per year or only in an emergency. Coldline Storage is optimized for this use case with very low per-GB storage cost. The higher retrieval cost for Coldline is acceptable because backups are rarely read in normal operations.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to set up a policy so that videos stored in a Cloud Storage Regional bucket are moved to Coldline after 90 days, and then deleted after one year from their creation. How should you set up the policy?

options.A: Use Object Lifecycle Management with Age conditions: SetStorageClass at 90 days, Delete at 275 days (365-90)
options.B: Use Object Lifecycle Management with Age conditions: SetStorageClass at 90 days, Delete at 365 days
options.C: Use gsutil rewrite and set the Delete action to 275 days (365-90)
options.D: Use gsutil rewrite and set the Delete action to 365 days

questions.explanation: Object Lifecycle Management Age conditions are measured from object creation date. SetStorageClass at 90 days transitions to Coldline after 90 days. Delete at 365 days deletes the object when it is 365 days old from creation — not 365-90=275 days. Both conditions use creation date as reference, so the Delete action should be 365, not 275.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to configure 10 Compute Engine instances for availability when maintenance occurs. These instances should attempt to automatically restart if they crash, and be highly available including during system maintenance. What should you do?

options.A: Create an instance template. Set 'Automatic Restart' to on. Set 'On-host maintenance' to Migrate VM instance. Add to an instance group.
options.B: Create an instance template. Set 'Automatic Restart' to off. Set 'On-host maintenance' to Terminate VM instances. Add to an instance group.
options.C: Create an instance group. Set the 'Autohealing' health check to healthy (HTTP).
options.D: Create an instance group. Verify that the 'Advanced creation options' setting for 'do not retry machine creation' is set to off.

questions.explanation: Automatic Restart=on ensures VMs restart after a crash or host maintenance. Live Migration (Migrate VM instance) moves the VM to another host during host maintenance without downtime — this is the key setting for high availability during maintenance. Terminate (B) would shut the VM down during maintenance instead of migrating it.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You are using multiple configurations for gcloud. You want to review the configured Kubernetes Engine cluster of an inactive configuration using the fewest possible steps. What should you do?

options.A: Use gcloud config configurations describe to review the output
options.B: Use gcloud config configurations activate and gcloud config list to review the output
options.C: Use kubectl config get-contexts to review the output
options.D: Use kubectl config use-context and kubectl config view to review the output

questions.explanation: kubectl config use-context switches to the desired cluster context, and kubectl config view shows the full kubeconfig including the cluster details. This achieves the goal using kubectl rather than gcloud, allowing you to inspect the cluster configuration without needing to activate the gcloud configuration.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You need to create a custom VPC with a single subnet. The subnet's range must be as large as possible. Which range should you use?
questions.explanation: GCP subnets must use RFC 1918 private IP ranges. Among the three RFC 1918 ranges, 10.0.0.0/8 provides the largest address space (16,777,216 addresses vs 1,048,576 for /12 and 65,536 for /16). 0.0.0.0/0 (A) is not a valid subnet address range for a VPC subnet.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You recently deployed a new version of an application to App Engine and then discovered a bug in the release. You need to immediately revert to the prior version of the application. What should you do?

options.A: Run gcloud app restore
options.B: On the App Engine page of the GCP Console, select the application and click Revert
options.C: On the App Engine Versions page of the GCP Console, route 100% of the traffic to the previous version
options.D: Deploy the original version as a separate application, then split traffic so the original version serves 100% of requests

questions.explanation: App Engine maintains all deployed versions. To rollback, you simply use traffic splitting to route 100% of traffic back to the previous version — no redeployment needed. This is the fastest and most reliable rollback method. gcloud app restore (A) doesn't exist as a command. Redeploying (D) is unnecessary and takes longer.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are creating a GKE cluster with cluster autoscaler enabled. You need to make sure that each node of the cluster will run a monitoring pod that sends container metrics to a third-party monitoring solution. What should you do?

options.A: Deploy the monitoring pod in a StatefulSet object
options.B: Deploy the monitoring pod in a DaemonSet object
options.C: Reference the monitoring pod in a Deployment object
options.D: Reference the monitoring pod in a cluster initializer at GKE cluster creation time

questions.explanation: DaemonSets automatically run a copy of a Pod on every node, including new nodes added by the cluster autoscaler. This ensures monitoring coverage as the cluster scales. A Deployment (C) runs a specified number of replicas, not one per node. StatefulSets (A) are for stateful applications requiring stable network identities.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You need to configure IAM access audit logging in BigQuery for external auditors. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Add the auditors group to the 'logging.viewer' and 'bigQuery.dataViewer' predefined IAM roles
options.B: Add the auditors group to two new custom IAM roles
options.C: Add the auditor user accounts to the 'logging.viewer' and 'bigQuery.dataViewer' predefined IAM roles
options.D: Add the auditor user accounts to two new custom IAM roles

questions.explanation: Google recommends managing IAM bindings using groups rather than individual user accounts — makes management easier when auditors change. Use predefined roles where they meet requirements rather than creating custom roles. logging.viewer lets them view audit logs; bigquery.dataViewer lets them see what data schemas were queried. Using the group (not individual accounts) follows best practices.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to configure Autohealing for network load balancing for a group of Compute Engine instances in multiple zones. You need to configure re-creation of VMs if they are unresponsive after 3 attempts of 10 seconds each. What should you do?

options.A: Create an HTTP load balancer with a backend configuration referencing an existing instance group. Set the health check to healthy (HTTP).
options.B: Create an HTTP load balancer with a backend configuration referencing an existing instance group. Define a balancing mode and set the maximum RPS to 10.
options.C: Create a Managed Instance Group. Set the Autohealing health check to healthy (HTTP).
options.D: Create a Managed Instance Group. Verify that the autoscaling setting is on.

questions.explanation: MIG Autohealing uses a health check to automatically detect and recreate unhealthy VMs. You configure the health check (including check interval and failure thresholds) on the MIG itself, not on the load balancer. Autoscaling (D) is about adding/removing instances based on load, not about replacing unhealthy ones.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You have a Linux VM that must connect to Cloud SQL. You created a service account with the appropriate access rights. You want to make sure that the VM uses this service account instead of the default Compute Engine service account. What should you do?

options.A: When creating the VM via the web console, specify the service account under the 'Identity and API Access' section
options.B: Download a JSON Private Key for the service account. On the Project Metadata, add that JSON as the value for the key compute-engine-service-account.
options.C: Download a JSON Private Key for the service account. On the Custom Metadata of the VM, add that JSON as the value for the key compute-engine-service-account.
options.D: Download a JSON Private Key for the service account. After creating the VM, ssh into the VM and save the JSON under ~/.gcloud/compute-engine-service-account.json.

questions.explanation: The correct way to associate a service account with a Compute Engine VM is to specify it during VM creation in the 'Identity and API Access' section (or via --service-account flag in gcloud). This is the cleanest, most secure approach. Downloading JSON keys (B, C, D) and placing them on the VM is an anti-pattern — it creates credential files that need to be secured separately.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You have one project called proj-sa where you manage all your service accounts. You want to use a service account from this project to take snapshots of VMs running in another project called proj-vm. What should you do?

options.A: Download the private key from the service account, and add it to each VM's custom metadata
options.B: Download the private key from the service account, and add the private key to each VM's SSH keys
options.C: Grant the service account the IAM role of Compute Storage Admin in the project called proj-vm
options.D: When creating the VMs, set the service account's API scope for Compute Engine to read/write

questions.explanation: Service accounts can be granted IAM roles in any project. By granting the service account from proj-sa the roles/compute.storageAdmin role in proj-vm, it gets the permissions to create snapshots of disks in that project. Service accounts are project-agnostic for IAM purposes — you don't need to move them or download keys.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You have one GCP account running in your default region/zone and another account running in a non-default region/zone. You want to start new Compute Engine instances in these two accounts using the command line. What should you do?

options.A: Create two configurations using gcloud config configurations create. Run gcloud config configurations activate to switch between accounts when running commands.
options.B: Create two configurations using gcloud config configurations create. Run gcloud configurations list to start the Compute Engine instances.
options.C: Activate two configurations using gcloud configurations activate. Run gcloud config list to start the Compute Engine instances.
options.D: Activate two configurations using gcloud configurations activate. Run gcloud configurations list to start the Compute Engine instances.

questions.explanation: gcloud named configurations let you switch between accounts and projects. You create one configuration per account/project pair and then activate the appropriate one before running commands. gcloud configurations list (B) only shows configurations; it doesn't start instances. The correct flow is: create configs → activate a config → run gcloud commands.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your operational team needs to manage a large number of Compute Engine instances. Each member needs only administrative access to the servers. Your security team wants to determine who accessed a given instance. What should you do?

options.A: Generate a new SSH key pair. Give the private key to each member. Configure the public key in the metadata of each instance.
options.B: Ask each member to generate a new SSH key pair and send you their public key. Use a configuration management tool to deploy those keys.
options.C: Ask each member to generate a new SSH key pair and add the public key to their Google account. Grant the 'compute.osAdminLogin' role to the Google group.
options.D: Generate a new SSH key pair. Give the private key to each member. Configure the public key as a project-wide public SSH key.

questions.explanation: OS Login integrates Linux VM login with Google accounts and IAM. Each user has their own SSH key pair linked to their Google account. The compute.osAdminLogin role grants sudo access. Audit logs track which specific Google account accessed each instance — satisfying the security team's requirement. Sharing a single key pair (A, D) makes attribution impossible.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to deploy an application packaged in a container image in a new project. The application exposes an HTTP endpoint and receives very few requests per day. You want to minimize costs. What should you do?

options.A: Deploy the container on Cloud Run
options.B: Deploy the container on Cloud Run on GKE
options.C: Deploy the container on App Engine Flexible
options.D: Deploy the container on GKE with cluster autoscaling and horizontal pod autoscaling enabled

questions.explanation: Cloud Run scales to zero when there are no requests and you pay only for the actual compute used during request processing. For a service receiving very few requests per day, this is dramatically cheaper than App Engine Flex (C) or GKE (D) which maintain running instances/nodes even when idle. Cloud Run on GKE (B) requires a GKE cluster which has ongoing node costs.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: Your development team needs a new Jenkins server for their project. You need to deploy the server using the fewest steps possible. What should you do?

options.A: Download and deploy the Jenkins Java WAR to App Engine Standard
options.B: Create a new Compute Engine instance and install Jenkins through the command line interface
options.C: Create a Kubernetes cluster on Compute Engine and create a deployment with the Jenkins Docker image
options.D: Use GCP Marketplace to launch the Jenkins solution

questions.explanation: GCP Marketplace provides pre-configured one-click deployment of common software solutions including Jenkins. This is the fastest path with the fewest steps — no manual installation, configuration, or image setup required. Installing Jenkins manually (B) or via Kubernetes (C) requires significantly more steps.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You are building an application that stores relational data from users. Users across the globe will use this application. Your CTO is concerned about scaling requirements because the user base size is unknown. Which storage solution should you use?

options.A: Cloud SQL
options.B: Cloud Spanner
options.C: Cloud Firestore
options.D: Cloud Datastore

questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner is the only relational database that scales horizontally to support global users with strong consistency. Cloud SQL (A) is single-region and doesn't scale horizontally for writes. Firestore (C) and Datastore (D) are NoSQL solutions that don't provide relational structure. When global scale + relational + unknown growth: Spanner.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a Dockerfile that you need to deploy on Kubernetes Engine. What should you do?

options.A: Use kubectl app deploy <dockerfilename>
options.B: Use gcloud app deploy <dockerfilename>
options.C: Create a docker image from the Dockerfile and upload it to Container Registry. Create a Deployment YAML file to point to that image. Use kubectl to create the deployment.
options.D: Create a docker image from the Dockerfile and upload it to Cloud Storage. Create a Deployment YAML file to point to that image. Use kubectl to create the deployment.

questions.explanation: The correct workflow for deploying a Dockerfile to GKE is: (1) build the Docker image, (2) push it to Container Registry (or Artifact Registry), (3) create a Kubernetes Deployment manifest referencing the image, (4) apply it with kubectl. Cloud Storage (D) is not a container registry. kubectl app deploy (A) and gcloud app deploy (B) are not valid for GKE.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You are building an application that will run in your data center. The application will use Google Cloud services like AutoML. You need to enable authentication to the APIs from your on-premises environment. What should you do?

options.A: Use service account credentials in your on-premises application
options.B: Use gcloud to create a key file for the service account that has appropriate permissions
options.C: Set up Cloud Interconnect between your data center and Google Cloud to enable authentication for your on-premises applications
options.D: In IAM & admin console, grant a user account permissions similar to the service account permissions and use this user account for authentication

questions.explanation: For on-premises applications authenticating to GCP APIs, the recommended approach is to create a service account, download its JSON key file using gcloud iam service-accounts keys create, and configure the application to use it via the GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS environment variable. Cloud Interconnect (C) provides network connectivity but not authentication. User accounts (D) are inappropriate for application-to-API authentication.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You host a static website on Cloud Storage. When users click links to PDF files, their browsers prompt them to save the file locally. Instead, you want PDFs to display within the browser window. What should you do?

options.A: Enable Cloud CDN on the website frontend
options.B: Enable 'Share publicly' on the PDF file objects
options.C: Set Content-Type metadata to application/pdf on the PDF file objects
options.D: Add a label to the storage bucket with a key of Content-Type and value of application/pdf

questions.explanation: The browser's behavior when opening a file is determined by the Content-Type HTTP header. When Cloud Storage serves a PDF with Content-Type: application/pdf, browsers display it inline. When the content type is incorrect (e.g., application/octet-stream), browsers trigger a download prompt. Labels (D) are for resource organization, not HTTP headers.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have an application that looks for its licensing server on the IP 10.0.3.21. You need to deploy the licensing server on Compute Engine without changing the application configuration. What should you do?

options.A: Reserve the IP 10.0.3.21 as a static internal IP address using gcloud and assign it to the licensing server
options.B: Reserve the IP 10.0.3.21 as a static public IP address using gcloud and assign it to the licensing server
options.C: Use the IP 10.0.3.21 as a custom ephemeral IP address and assign it to the licensing server
options.D: Start the licensing server with an automatic ephemeral IP address, and then promote it to a static internal IP address

questions.explanation: 10.0.3.21 is a private RFC 1918 IP address, so it must be reserved as a static internal IP. Static internal IP reservations guarantee that the Compute Engine instance always gets this specific address. Ephemeral IPs (C) are not guaranteed and could change. A static public IP (B) would not use a private 10.x.x.x address.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are deploying an application to App Engine. You want the number of instances to scale based on request rate. You need at least 3 unoccupied instances at all times. Which scaling type should you use?

options.A: Manual Scaling with 3 instances
options.B: Basic Scaling with min_instances set to 3
options.C: Basic Scaling with max_instances set to 3
options.D: Automatic Scaling with min_idle_instances set to 3

questions.explanation: Automatic Scaling in App Engine supports min_idle_instances, which keeps a minimum number of unoccupied (warm) instances ready to handle traffic spikes. This satisfies 'scale based on request rate AND keep at least 3 unoccupied instances.' Basic Scaling (B, C) doesn't scale based on request rate continuously. Manual Scaling (A) doesn't scale at all.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to update a Deployment Manager deployment without any resource downtime. Which command should you use?

options.A: gcloud deployment-manager deployments create --config <config>
options.B: gcloud deployment-manager deployments update --config <config>
options.C: gcloud deployment-manager resources create --config <config>
options.D: gcloud deployment-manager resources update --config <config>

questions.explanation: gcloud deployment-manager deployments update applies changes to an existing deployment incrementally, updating only the resources that changed. It supports --preview to show changes before applying. The create command (A) would fail if the deployment already exists or would create duplicates.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You are running an application on a MIG and have autoscaling enabled. The autoscaling policy adds instances when CPU goes above 80%. VMs take around 3 minutes to become available. You observe that autoscaling adds more instances than necessary. What should you do?

options.A: Set the maximum number of instances to 1
options.B: Decrease the maximum number of instances to 3
options.C: Use a TCP health check instead of an HTTP health check
options.D: Increase the initial delay of the HTTP health check to 200 seconds

questions.explanation: The initial delay tells the MIG how long to wait before checking a new instance's health. If it's too short (default 30s) and VMs take 3 minutes to be available, the MIG may consider new instances unhealthy and create even more. Setting the initial delay to 200 seconds (longer than the 3-minute startup) prevents the autoscaler from over-provisioning while instances are still initializing.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have 32 GB of data in a single file to upload to a Nearline Storage bucket. The WAN connection is rated at 1 Gbps and you are the only one using it. You want to use as much of the rated 1 Gbps as possible. How should you upload the file?

options.A: Use the GCP Console to transfer the file instead of gsutil
options.B: Enable parallel composite uploads using gsutil on the file transfer
options.C: Decrease the TCP window size on the machine initiating the transfer
options.D: Change the storage class of the bucket from Nearline to Multi-Regional

questions.explanation: gsutil parallel composite uploads break the file into chunks and upload them simultaneously using multiple connections, allowing you to saturate the available bandwidth. A single TCP connection rarely utilizes 1 Gbps due to TCP congestion control. The GCP Console (A) uses a single HTTP connection. Decreasing TCP window size (C) would reduce throughput. Storage class (D) doesn't affect upload speed.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You want to verify the IAM users and roles assigned within a GCP project named my-project. What should you do?

options.A: Run gcloud iam roles list. Review the output section.
options.B: Run gcloud iam service-accounts list. Review the output section.
options.C: Navigate to the project and then to the IAM section in the GCP Console. Review the members and roles.
options.D: Navigate to the project and then to the Roles section in the GCP Console. Review the roles and status.

questions.explanation: The IAM section in the GCP Console shows all principals (users, groups, service accounts) and their assigned roles for the project. gcloud iam roles list (A) shows available roles, not who has been assigned them. gcloud iam service-accounts list (B) shows only service accounts, not all IAM bindings.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to select and configure a cost-effective solution for relational data on GCP. You are working with a small set of operational data in one geographic location. You need to support point-in-time recovery. What should you do?

options.A: Select Cloud SQL (MySQL). Verify that the enable binary logging option is selected.
options.B: Select Cloud SQL (MySQL). Select the create failover replicas option.
options.C: Select Cloud Spanner. Set up your instance with 2 nodes.
options.D: Select Cloud Spanner. Set up your instance as multi-regional.

questions.explanation: For a small operational dataset in one region with point-in-time recovery (PITR), Cloud SQL is the cost-effective choice.
