PITR in Cloud SQL requires binary logging to be enabled — it records every transaction, allowing you to restore to any point in time. Failover replicas (B) provide high availability, not PITR. Cloud Spanner (C, D) is far more expensive and unnecessary for small datasets.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are hosting an application on bare-metal servers in your data center. The application needs access to Cloud Storage. Security policies prevent servers from having public IP addresses or internet access. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Use nslookup to get the IP address for storage.googleapis.com. Negotiate with the security team to give servers a public IP address.
options.B: Using Cloud VPN, create a VPN tunnel to a VPC. Install a Squid proxy on a Compute Engine instance. Configure servers to use that proxy.
options.C: Use Migrate for Compute Engine to migrate those servers. Create an internal load balancer that uses storage.googleapis.com as backend.
options.D: Using Cloud VPN or Interconnect, create a tunnel to a VPC. Use Cloud Router to advertise 199.36.153.4/30. Configure DNS to resolve *.googleapis.com as CNAME to restricted.googleapis.com.

questions.explanation: Private Google Access for on-premises hosts allows access to Google APIs over a VPN/Interconnect tunnel using the restricted.googleapis.com VIP (199.36.153.4/30). Cloud Router advertises this IP range to on-premises. DNS resolves *.googleapis.com to restricted.googleapis.com, routing traffic through the private path without public internet access.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your company has a BigQuery data warehouse. Your data science team changes frequently. You need to allow members to perform queries. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Create an IAM entry for each data scientist's user account. Assign the BigQuery jobUser role to the group.
options.B: Create an IAM entry for each data scientist's user account. Assign the BigQuery dataViewer role to the group.
options.C: Create a dedicated Google group in Cloud Identity. Add each data scientist's user account. Assign the BigQuery jobUser role to the group.
options.D: Create a dedicated Google group in Cloud Identity. Add each data scientist's user account. Assign the BigQuery dataViewer role to the group.

questions.explanation: BigQuery jobUser role allows running queries (creating and managing jobs) but doesn't grant access to data. When combined with appropriate dataset-level permissions, this allows team members to run queries. Using a Google group (not individual accounts) is recommended because when team members change, you only update group membership, not IAM policies. dataViewer (D) allows reading data but not running jobs.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You have a single VPC with a subnet in us-central1. There is a Compute Engine instance hosting an application. You need to deploy a new instance in europe-west1 that needs access to the application. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Create a subnetwork in the same VPC in europe-west1. Create the new instance in the new subnetwork and use the first instance's private address as the endpoint.
options.B: Create a VPC and subnetwork in europe-west1. Expose the application with an internal load balancer. Create the new instance and use the load balancer's address.
options.C: Create a subnetwork in the same VPC in europe-west1. Use Cloud VPN to connect the two subnetworks. Create the new instance and use the first instance's private address.
options.D: Create a VPC and subnetwork in europe-west1. Peer the 2 VPCs. Create the new instance and use the first instance's private address.

questions.explanation: A single VPC in GCP is global — it can have subnets in different regions. Instances in different regional subnets of the same VPC can communicate via internal IP without additional configuration. Creating a new subnet in europe-west1 within the same VPC is the simplest approach and doesn't require VPN, VPC peering, or load balancers.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You are building a pipeline to process time-series data. You need to select GCP services for ingestion, processing, storage, and analytical querying. Which combination is correct?

options.A: Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud Dataflow, Cloud Datastore, BigQuery
options.B: Firebase Messages, Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud Spanner, BigQuery
options.C: Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Cloud Bigtable
options.D: Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud Dataflow, Cloud Bigtable, BigQuery

questions.explanation: The standard GCP streaming pipeline: Pub/Sub (ingestion/messaging) → Dataflow (stream processing/ETL) → Bigtable (high-throughput time-series storage for operational access) → BigQuery (analytical queries and reporting). Cloud Datastore (A) is not suited for time-series data. Cloud Storage (C) can't provide low-latency time-series queries.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: For analysis purposes, you need to send all logs from all Compute Engine instances to a BigQuery dataset called platform-logs. You have already installed the Cloud Logging agent on all instances. You want to minimize cost. What should you do?

options.A: Give the BigQuery Data Editor role on platform-logs to the service accounts used by instances. Update instances' metadata to add logs-destination: bq://platform-logs.
options.B: In Cloud Logging, create a logs export with a Cloud Pub/Sub topic called logs as a sink. Create a Cloud Function triggered by messages to insert Compute Engine logs in the dataset.
options.C: In Cloud Logging, create a filter to view only Compute Engine logs. Click Create Export. Choose BigQuery as Sink Service, and platform-logs as Sink Destination.
options.D: Create a Cloud Function that has the BigQuery User role on platform-logs. Configure this Cloud Function to create a BigQuery Job that queries compute.logs. Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger it once a day.

questions.explanation: Cloud Logging log sinks are the direct, minimal-cost way to export logs to BigQuery. You create a filter for Compute Engine logs and a sink pointing to the BigQuery dataset. Log entries are exported in near-real time without additional services. The Pub/Sub + Cloud Function approach (B) adds unnecessary complexity and cost.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to deploy an application on Cloud Run that processes messages from a Cloud Pub/Sub topic. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Create a Cloud Function that uses a Cloud Pub/Sub trigger. Call your Cloud Run application from the Cloud Function for every message.
options.B: Grant the Pub/Sub Subscriber role to the service account used by Cloud Run. Create a Pub/Sub subscription. Make your application pull messages.
options.C: Create a service account. Give the Cloud Run Invoker role to that service account for your Cloud Run application. Create a Pub/Sub subscription that uses that service account and uses your Cloud Run application as the push endpoint.
options.D: Deploy your application on Cloud Run with the connectivity set to Internal. Create a Pub/Sub subscription. In the same GKE cluster, deploy a container that sends messages to your application.

questions.explanation: The Google-recommended pattern is to use Pub/Sub push subscriptions to trigger Cloud Run. Pub/Sub sends HTTP POST requests to the Cloud Run URL, authenticated using a service account with the Cloud Run Invoker role. This is fully managed, scales automatically, and requires no polling. Pull subscriptions (B) require the application to continuously poll, which is less efficient.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: Your GKE container emitted a huge number of logs, resulting in higher costs. You want to disable the logs quickly using the minimum number of steps. What should you do?

options.A: Go to the Logs ingestion window in Cloud Logging, and disable the log source for the GKE container resource
options.B: Go to the Logs ingestion window in Cloud Logging, and disable the log source for the GKE Cluster Operations resource
options.C: Go to the GKE console, delete existing clusters, recreate a new cluster, and clear the option to enable legacy Stackdriver Logging
options.D: Go to the GKE console, delete existing clusters, recreate a new cluster, and clear the option to enable legacy Stackdriver Monitoring

questions.explanation: Cloud Logging's log ingestion controls allow you to disable specific log sources without recreating infrastructure. Disabling the GKE container resource logs stops collecting container logs immediately with a single change — the fewest possible steps. Deleting and recreating clusters (C, D) would cause downtime and is far more disruptive.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a microservice deployed on GKE. You need to refactor its configuration so that the database password is not stored in plain text in the YAML file. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Store the database password inside the Docker image of the container
options.B: Store the database password inside a Secret object. Modify the YAML file to populate the DB_PASSWORD environment variable from the Secret.
options.C: Store the database password inside a ConfigMap object. Modify the YAML file to populate the DB_PASSWORD environment variable from the ConfigMap.
options.D: Store the database password in a file inside a Kubernetes persistent volume, and use a persistent volume claim to mount the volume to the container.

questions.explanation: Kubernetes Secrets are designed specifically for sensitive data like passwords, OAuth tokens, and SSH keys. They are stored base64-encoded in etcd and can be injected into containers as environment variables or mounted as files. ConfigMaps (C) are for non-sensitive configuration data. Storing secrets in Docker images (A) or persistent volumes (D) is an anti-pattern.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You have an application running in GKE. You have a Compute Engine instance in the same region, but in another VPC called gce-network, that has no overlapping IP ranges with the first VPC. This instance needs to connect to the GKE application on a TCP endpoint. You want to minimize effort. What should you do?

options.A: In GKE, create a Service of type LoadBalancer. Set the service's externalTrafficPolicy to Cluster. Configure the Compute Engine instance to use the address of the load balancer.
options.B: In GKE, create a Service of type NodePort. Create a Compute Engine instance proxy with 2 network interfaces. Use iptables on this instance to forward traffic.
options.C: In GKE, create a Service of type LoadBalancer with annotation cloud.google.com/load-balancer-type: Internal. Peer the two VPCs. Configure the Compute Engine instance to use the internal load balancer address.
options.D: In GKE, create a Service of type LoadBalancer. Add a Cloud Armor Security Policy to the load balancer that whitelists the internal IPs. Configure the Compute Engine instance to use the load balancer address.

questions.explanation: An external LoadBalancer Service in GKE provisions a public IP. The Compute Engine instance in the other VPC can reach it via the public internet. This minimizes effort — no VPC peering, no proxy, no Cloud Armor needed. An internal LB (C) with VPC peering would work but requires more steps. Cloud Armor (D) adds security but doesn't simplify connectivity.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You are using Container Registry to store container images in a separate project. In another project, you want to create a GKE cluster. You want to ensure that Kubernetes can download images from Container Registry. What should you do?

options.A: In the project where the images are stored, grant the Storage Object Viewer IAM role to the service account used by the Kubernetes nodes
options.B: When you create the GKE cluster, choose the 'Allow full access to all Cloud APIs' option under 'Access scopes'
options.C: Create a service account, and give it access to Cloud Storage. Create a P12 key for this service account and use it as an imagePullSecrets in Kubernetes.
options.D: Configure the ACLs on each image in Cloud Storage to give read-only access to the default Compute Engine service account

questions.explanation: Container Registry stores images in Cloud Storage. GKE nodes pull images using their node service account. Granting Storage Object Viewer to the GKE node service account in the registry project allows cross-project image pulling. This is more secure than 'full access to all Cloud APIs' (B) which grants excessive permissions.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You deployed a new application inside your GKE cluster. One of the pods is still in PENDING status. You want to find out why. What should you do?

options.A: Review details of the myapp-service Service object and check for error messages
options.B: Review details of the myapp-deployment Deployment object and check for error messages
options.C: Review details of the pending Pod and check for warning messages
options.D: View logs of the container in the pending pod and check for warning messages

questions.explanation: A Pod in Pending status hasn't been scheduled yet, so the container hasn't started and there are no container logs (D). Pod events and conditions (shown in kubectl describe pod) reveal why a pod is Pending: insufficient resources, node affinity mismatches, unbound PVCs, or image pull issues. The Service (A) and Deployment (B) objects don't show individual pod scheduling failures.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are setting up a Windows VM on Compute Engine and want to make sure you can log in to the VM via RDP. What should you do?

options.A: After the VM has been created, use your Google Account credentials to log in to the VM
options.B: After the VM has been created, use gcloud compute reset-windows-password to retrieve the login credentials for the VM
options.C: When creating the VM, add metadata to the instance using 'windows-password' as the key and a password as the value
options.D: After the VM has been created, download the JSON Private Key for the default Compute Engine service account

questions.explanation: gcloud compute reset-windows-password generates and sets a new Windows password for a specified user on a Windows VM, then outputs the credentials. This is the Google-recommended way to set up RDP access for Windows VMs on Compute Engine. Google Account credentials (A) don't work for Windows VM RDP login. There's no 'windows-password' metadata key (C).

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You want to configure an SSH connection to a single Compute Engine instance for users in the dev1 group. This instance is the only resource in the project that dev1 users should be able to connect to. What should you do?

options.A: Set metadata to enable-oslogin=true for the instance. Grant the dev1 group the compute.osLogin role. Direct them to use Cloud Shell to SSH.
options.B: Set metadata to enable-oslogin=true for the instance. Set the service account to no service account for that instance. Direct them to Cloud Shell.
options.C: Enable block project wide keys for the instance. Generate an SSH key for each user in the dev1 group. Distribute keys and direct users to third-party tools.
options.D: Enable block project wide keys for the instance. Generate an SSH key and associate the key with that instance. Distribute the key to dev1 users.

questions.explanation: OS Login at the instance level (enable-oslogin=true in instance metadata) enables IAM-based SSH access. Granting compute.osLogin to the dev1 group allows them to SSH into this specific instance. This is scoped to the instance only (not project-wide), satisfying the requirement. Distributing shared keys (C, D) makes attribution difficult and is not a Google best practice.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You need to produce a list of the enabled GCP APIs for a project named my-project using gcloud in Cloud Shell. What should you do?

options.A: Run gcloud projects list to get the project ID, and then run gcloud services list --project <PROJECT_ID>
options.B: Run gcloud init to set the current project to my-project, and then run gcloud services list --available
options.C: Run gcloud info to view the account value, and then run gcloud services list --account <ACCOUNT>
options.D: Run gcloud projects describe to verify the project value, and then run gcloud services list --available

questions.explanation: gcloud services list --project <PROJECT_ID> lists the enabled APIs for the specified project. You first need the project ID (gcloud projects list). The --available flag (B, D) lists all APIs that could be enabled, not what is currently enabled. Filtering by account (C) is not how gcloud services list works.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are building a new version of an App Engine application. You want to test the new version with 1% of users before completely switching. What should you do?

options.A: Deploy a new version of your application in GKE instead of App Engine and use GCP Console to split traffic
options.B: Deploy a new version in Compute Engine instead of App Engine and use GCP Console to split traffic
options.C: Deploy a new version as a separate app in App Engine. Configure App Engine to split traffic between the two apps.
options.D: Deploy a new version of your application in App Engine. Then go to App Engine settings in GCP Console and split traffic between the current version and newly deployed versions.

questions.explanation: App Engine's built-in traffic splitting allows routing a percentage of traffic to different versions of the same service. You deploy the new version (which doesn't become the default) and then configure traffic splitting in the Console or via gcloud. You don't need a separate App Engine app (C) or a different compute platform (A, B).

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are using GKE with autoscaling to host a new application. You want to expose this application to the public using HTTPS on a public IP address. What should you do?

options.A: Create a Kubernetes Service of type NodePort for your application, and a Kubernetes Ingress to expose this Service via a Cloud Load Balancer
options.B: Create a Kubernetes Service of type ClusterIP for your application. Configure the public DNS name of your application using the IP of this Service.
options.C: Create a Kubernetes Service of type NodePort to expose the application on port 443 of each node. Configure the public DNS name using the IP of every node.
options.D: Create a HAProxy pod in the cluster to load-balance the traffic to all the pods. Forward the public traffic to HAProxy with an iptable rule.

questions.explanation: The correct pattern for public HTTPS access in GKE is: NodePort Service (to expose pods on cluster nodes) + Ingress with a TLS termination (which provisions a Cloud HTTPS Load Balancer). ClusterIP (B) is only accessible within the cluster. Pointing DNS to every node IP (C) is fragile. HAProxy (D) is a custom solution that doesn't scale well.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You need to enable traffic between multiple groups of Compute Engine instances currently running in two different GCP projects. Each group runs in its own VPC. What should you do?

options.A: Verify that both projects are in a GCP Organization. Create a new VPC and add all instances.
options.B: Verify that both projects are in a GCP Organization. Share the VPC from one project and request that the Compute Engine instances in the other project use this Shared VPC.
options.C: Verify that you are the Project Administrator of both projects. Create two new VPCs and add all instances.
options.D: Verify that you are the Project Administrator of both projects. Create a new VPC and add all instances.

questions.explanation: Shared VPC is the recommended approach for enabling communication between resources in different projects within the same organization. The host project shares its VPC, and service projects can create VMs in that shared VPC. This centralizes network management. VPC Peering would also work but is not among the options — Shared VPC is the better answer here.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You want to add a new auditor to a GCP project. The auditor should be allowed to read, but not modify, all project items. How should you configure the auditor's permissions?

options.A: Create a custom role with view-only project permissions. Add the user's account to the custom role.
options.B: Create a custom role with view-only service permissions. Add the user's account to the custom role.
options.C: Select the built-in IAM project Viewer role. Add the user's account to this role.
options.D: Select the built-in IAM service Viewer role. Add the user's account to this role.

questions.explanation: The built-in roles/viewer role grants read-only access to all resources in the project without any modification capabilities. Using predefined roles is preferred over creating custom roles when they meet the requirements — it reduces management overhead. Creating a custom view-only role (A) would duplicate functionality that already exists in the predefined Viewer role.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are operating a GKE cluster where different teams run non-production workloads. Your ML team needs access to Nvidia Tesla P100 GPUs to train their models. You want to minimize effort and cost. What should you do?

options.A: Ask your ML team to add the accelerator: gpu annotation to their pod specification
options.B: Recreate all the nodes of the GKE cluster to enable GPUs on all of them
options.C: Create your own Kubernetes cluster on top of Compute Engine with nodes that have GPUs
options.D: Add a new GPU-enabled node pool to the GKE cluster. Ask your ML team to add the cloud.google.com/gke-accelerator: nvidia-tesla-p100 nodeSelector to their pod specification.

questions.explanation: Adding a GPU node pool to an existing cluster allows only the ML team's pods (via nodeSelector) to use GPU nodes, while other workloads continue using the existing CPU node pool. The node pool can scale to zero when not in use, minimizing cost. Recreating all nodes (B) would add GPU cost to all workloads. A separate cluster (C) requires more management overhead.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: Your VMs are running in a subnet with mask 255.255.255.240. The current subnet has no more free IP addresses and you require an additional 10 IP addresses for new VMs. The existing and new VMs should all be able to reach each other without additional routes. What should you do?

options.A: Use gcloud to expand the IP range of the current subnet
options.B: Delete the subnet, and recreate it using a wider range of IP addresses
options.C: Create a new project. Use Shared VPC to share the current network with the new project.
options.D: Create a new subnet with the same starting IP but a wider range to overwrite the current subnet.

questions.explanation: GCP allows expanding (not shrinking) the IP range of an existing subnet using gcloud compute networks subnets expand-ip-range. This is a non-destructive operation and maintains all existing VMs and their IPs. Deleting and recreating the subnet (B) would remove all existing VMs' network connectivity. Option D is not possible — you can't overwrite an existing subnet.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your organization uses Google Workspace for communication. All users in your organization have a Google Workspace account. You want to grant some users access to your Cloud Platform project. What should you do?

options.A: Enable Cloud Identity in the GCP Console for your domain
options.B: Grant them the required IAM roles using their Google Workspace email address
options.C: Create a CSV sheet with all users' email addresses. Use gcloud to convert them into GCP accounts.
options.D: In the Google Workspace console, add the users to a special group called cloud-console-users@yourdomain.com

questions.explanation: Google Workspace accounts (formerly G Suite) are Google accounts and can be directly used in GCP IAM. You simply grant IAM roles to their email addresses — no additional setup, conversion, or special groups required. Google Workspace users are already recognized as Google accounts in GCP.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a large 5-TB AVRO file stored in a Cloud Storage bucket. Your analysts are proficient only in SQL and need access to the data. You want a cost-effective way to complete their request as soon as possible. What should you do?

options.A: Load data in Cloud Datastore and run a SQL query against it
options.B: Create a BigQuery table and load data in BigQuery. Run a SQL query on this table.
options.C: Create external tables in BigQuery that point to Cloud Storage buckets and run a SQL query on these external tables.
options.D: Create a Hadoop cluster and copy the AVRO file to NDFS by compressing it. Load the file in a hive table and provide access to your analysts.

questions.explanation: BigQuery External Tables allow querying files in Cloud Storage directly using SQL without importing or moving data. AVRO is a natively supported format. This is the fastest option (no data loading needed) and cost-effective (no storage duplication). Loading into BigQuery (B) would work but takes time and doubles storage cost. Hadoop (D) is complex and time-consuming.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to verify that a Google Cloud Platform service account was created at a particular time. What should you do?

options.A: Filter the Activity log to view the Configuration category. Filter the Resource type to Service Account.
options.B: Filter the Activity log to view the Configuration category. Filter the Resource type to Google Project.
options.C: Filter the Activity log to view the Data Access category. Filter the Resource type to Service Account.
options.D: Filter the Activity log to view the Data Access category. Filter the Resource type to Google Project.

questions.explanation: Creating a service account is an admin/configuration action, not a data access action. Cloud Audit Logs Admin Activity (Configuration category) records resource creation events. Filtering by Resource type 'Service Account' shows when service accounts were created, modified, or deleted. Data Access logs (C) record read/write operations on data within resources.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You deployed an LDAP server on Compute Engine that is reachable via TLS through port 636 using UDP. You want to make sure it is reachable by clients over that port. What should you do?

options.A: Add the network tag allow-udp-636 to the VM instance running the LDAP server
options.B: Create a route called allow-udp-636 and set the next hop to be the VM instance running the LDAP server
options.C: Add a network tag of your choice to the instance. Create a firewall rule to allow ingress on UDP port 636 for that network tag.
options.D: Add a network tag of your choice to the instance running the LDAP server. Create a firewall rule to allow egress on UDP port 636 for that network tag.

questions.explanation: Firewall rules in GCP use network tags to target specific VMs. The correct approach is: (1) add a custom tag to the VM, (2) create an INGRESS firewall rule that allows UDP port 636 traffic targeting that tag. The tag name itself (A) has no special meaning — you must create an explicit firewall rule. Egress (D) controls outbound traffic from the VM, not inbound.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You need to set a budget alert for use of Compute Engine services on one of three GCP projects linked to a single billing account. What should you do?

options.A: Verify that you are the project billing administrator. Select the associated billing account and create a budget and alert for the appropriate project.
options.B: Verify that you are the project billing administrator. Select the associated billing account and create a budget and a custom alert.
options.C: Verify that you are the project administrator. Select the associated billing account and create a budget for the appropriate project.
options.D: Verify that you are project administrator. Select the associated billing account and create a budget and a custom alert.

questions.explanation: Budget alerts are managed at the billing account level. The Billing Account Administrator or Billing Account Costs Manager role is needed to create budgets. You can scope a budget to a specific project and service (Compute Engine) to get targeted alerts. Project administrator (C, D) doesn't have billing account management permissions.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are migrating a production-critical on-premises application that requires 96 vCPUs to perform its task. You want to make sure the application runs in a similar environment on GCP. What should you do?

options.A: When creating the VM, use machine type n1-standard-96
options.B: When creating the VM, use Intel Skylake as the CPU platform
options.C: Create the VM using default settings. Use gcloud to modify the running instance to have 96 vCPUs.
options.D: Start the VM using default settings, and adjust as you go based on Rightsizing Recommendations.

questions.explanation: n1-standard-96 is a predefined machine type with 96 vCPUs and 360 GB of memory — matching the on-premises requirement directly. You cannot modify the number of vCPUs on a running VM (C) — it requires stopping the VM first. Rightsizing Recommendations (D) are for optimization after deployment, not initial sizing. Intel Skylake (B) specifies CPU architecture, not vCPU count.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to configure a solution for archiving data in a Cloud Storage bucket. Data with multiple versions should be archived after 30 days. Previous versions are accessed once a month for reporting and occasionally updated at month-end. What should you do?

options.A: Add a bucket lifecycle rule that archives data with newer versions after 30 days to Coldline Storage
options.B: Add a bucket lifecycle rule that archives data with newer versions after 30 days to Nearline Storage
options.C: Add a bucket lifecycle rule that archives data from regional storage after 30 days to Coldline Storage
options.D: Add a bucket lifecycle rule that archives data from regional storage after 30 days to Nearline Storage

questions.explanation: Previous versions accessed once a month and occasionally updated = Nearline (accessed less than once per month, 30-day minimum). Coldline (A, C) requires less than once per quarter and has a 90-day minimum storage — if versions are updated at month-end, they'd incur early deletion fees. The lifecycle rule should target older versions (using IsLive: false condition), not regional storage class (C, D).

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: Your company's infrastructure is on-premises but running at maximum capacity. You want to burst to Google Cloud. The workloads on GCP must communicate with on-premises workloads using private IP ranges. What should you do?

options.A: In Google Cloud, configure the VPC as a host for Shared VPC
options.B: In Google Cloud, configure the VPC for VPC Network Peering
options.C: Create bastion hosts both on-premises and on Google Cloud. Configure both as proxy servers using their public IP addresses.
options.D: Set up Cloud VPN between the infrastructure on-premises and Google Cloud

questions.explanation: Cloud VPN creates a secure tunnel between on-premises and GCP over the internet, allowing private IP communication. This is the standard hybrid connectivity solution for cloud bursting. Shared VPC (A) is for multi-project GCP communication. VPC Peering (B) is between two GCP VPCs. Bastion hosts (C) require public IPs and don't create seamless private IP connectivity.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to support compliance objectives for data from one geographic location. This data is archived after 30 days and needs to be accessed annually. What should you do?

options.A: Select Multi-Regional Storage. Add a bucket lifecycle rule that archives data after 30 days to Coldline Storage.
options.B: Select Multi-Regional Storage. Add a bucket lifecycle rule that archives data after 30 days to Nearline Storage.
options.C: Select Regional Storage. Add a bucket lifecycle rule that archives data after 30 days to Nearline Storage.
options.D: Select Regional Storage. Add a bucket lifecycle rule that archives data after 30 days to Coldline Storage.

questions.explanation: For data residency compliance in one geographic location, use Regional Storage (keeps data in a single region). Annual access frequency (once per year) maps to Coldline (accessed less than once per quarter). Nearline (C) would be appropriate for monthly access. Multi-Regional (A, B) stores data across multiple regions, which violates the single-location compliance requirement.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: Your company uses BigQuery for data warehousing. Your CIO wants you to examine all datasets across 1000+ datasets to find tables that contain an employee_ssn column. You want to minimize effort. What should you do?

options.A: Go to Data Catalog and search for employee_ssn in the search box
options.B: Write a shell script that uses the bq command line tool to loop through all projects in your organization
options.C: Write a script that loops through all projects and runs a query on INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS view to find the employee_ssn column
options.D: Write a Cloud Dataflow job that loops through all projects and runs a query on INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS view

questions.explanation: Google Cloud Data Catalog automatically catalogs metadata from BigQuery datasets, tables, and columns across your organization. Searching for 'employee_ssn' in Data Catalog instantly shows all tables containing that column across all projects. This requires zero custom code — far less effort than writing scripts (B, C) or Dataflow jobs (D) to scan INFORMATION_SCHEMA across hundreds of projects.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You create a Deployment with 2 replicas in a GKE cluster with a single preemptible node pool. After a few minutes, you use kubectl to examine the status of your Pods and observe that one of them is still in Pending status. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: The pending Pod's resource requests are too large to fit on a single node of the cluster
options.B: Too many Pods are already running in the cluster and there are not enough resources left
options.C: The node pool is configured with a service account that does not have permission to pull the container image
options.D: The pending Pod was originally scheduled on a node that has been preempted. It is currently being rescheduled on a new node.

questions.explanation: Preemptible VMs can be terminated by Google with 30 seconds notice. A single-node preemptible pool means if the node gets preempted, all pods on it enter Pending state while GKE provisions a replacement node. This is a known risk of using single-node pools with preemptible VMs. Resource issues (A, B) would cause Pending from the start, not after 'a few minutes'.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You want to find out when users were added to Cloud Spanner IAM roles on your GCP project. What should you do in the GCP Console?

options.A: Open the Cloud Spanner console to review configurations
options.B: Open the IAM & admin console to review IAM policies for Cloud Spanner roles
options.C: Go to the Cloud Monitoring console and review information for Cloud Spanner
options.D: Go to the Cloud Logging console, review admin activity logs, and filter them for Cloud Spanner IAM roles

questions.explanation: IAM policy changes (like adding users to roles) are recorded in Cloud Audit Logs as Admin Activity logs. Filtering Cloud Logging for Cloud Spanner IAM-related events shows exactly when users were added to Spanner roles and by whom. The IAM console (B) shows the current state, not historical changes. Cloud Monitoring (C) tracks metrics, not IAM changes.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: Your company implemented BigQuery as an enterprise data warehouse. Query costs are very high and you need to control costs. Which TWO methods should you use? (Choose two.)

options.A: Split the users from business units to multiple projects
options.B: Apply a user- or project-level custom query quota for BigQuery data warehouse
options.C: Create separate copies of your BigQuery data warehouse for each business unit
options.D: Split your BigQuery data warehouse into multiple data warehouses for each business unit
options.E: Change your BigQuery query model from on-demand to flat rate. Apply the appropriate number of slots to each Project.

questions.explanation: Two effective approaches: (B) Custom quotas limit the bytes scanned per user or project per day, preventing any single user from incurring large costs. (E) Flat-rate pricing provides predictable costs by purchasing dedicated slots — once the slot capacity is used, queries queue rather than incur additional cost. Splitting users to projects (A) or creating data copies (C, D) increases costs rather than reducing them.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You are building a product on top of GKE. For each of your customers, a Pod is running in that cluster, and your customers can run arbitrary code inside their Pod. You want to maximize the isolation between your customers' Pods. What should you do?

options.A: Use Binary Authorization and whitelist only the container images used by your customers' Pods
options.B: Use the Container Analysis API to detect vulnerabilities in the containers used by your customers' Pods
options.C: Create a GKE node pool with a sandbox type configured to gvisor. Add the parameter runtimeClassName: gvisor to the specification of your customers' Pods.
options.D: Use the cos_containerd image for your GKE nodes. Add a nodeSelector with the value cloud.google.com/gke-os-distribution: cos_containerd.

questions.explanation: gVisor is a container sandboxing technology that provides an additional isolation layer between containers and the host kernel. It intercepts system calls within a user-space kernel, preventing malicious code from directly accessing the host kernel. This is specifically designed for multi-tenant environments where customers run arbitrary code. Binary Authorization (A) controls which images can be deployed but doesn't isolate running containers.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your finance team wants to view the billing report for your projects. You want to make sure that the finance team does not get additional permissions to the project. What should you do?

options.A: Add the group for the finance team to roles/billing.user role
options.B: Add the group for the finance team to roles/billing.admin role
options.C: Add the group for the finance team to roles/billing.viewer role
options.D: Add the group for the finance team to roles/billing.projectManager role

questions.explanation: roles/billing.viewer grants read-only access to billing accounts, allowing the finance team to view billing reports, invoices, and cost analysis without any ability to modify billing configurations, create budgets, or change project links. roles/billing.user (A) allows linking projects to billing accounts. roles/billing.admin (B) grants full control. Only viewer meets the 'view only' requirement.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your organization has strict requirements to control access to GCP projects. You need to enable SREs to approve requests from the Google Cloud support team when an SRE opens a support case. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Add your SREs to roles/iam.roleAdmin role
options.B: Add your SREs to roles/accessapproval.approver role
options.C: Add your SREs to a group and then add this group to roles/iam.roleAdmin role
options.D: Add your SREs to a group and then add this group to roles/accessapproval.approver role

questions.explanation: Access Approval is a GCP feature that requires explicit approval before Google support staff can access your resources. roles/accessapproval.approver grants the ability to approve or deny these access requests. Using a group (not individual accounts) is the Google-recommended practice for IAM management. roles/iam.roleAdmin (A, C) grants the ability to create and manage IAM roles, not to approve support access.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to host an application on a Compute Engine instance in a project shared with other teams. You want to prevent the other teams from accidentally causing downtime on that application. Which feature should you use?

options.A: Use a Shielded VM
options.B: Use a Preemptible VM
options.C: Use a sole-tenant node
options.D: Enable deletion protection on the instance

questions.explanation: Deletion protection prevents a Compute Engine instance from being accidentally deleted. When enabled, attempts to delete the instance will fail until deletion protection is removed. This protects against accidental downtime caused by other team members. Shielded VMs (A) protect against malicious software. Sole-tenant nodes (C) are for hardware isolation. Preemptible VMs (B) are actually more likely to cause downtime.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your organization needs to grant users access to query datasets in BigQuery but prevent them from accidentally deleting the datasets. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Add users to roles/bigquery.user role only, instead of roles/bigquery.dataOwner
options.B: Add users to roles/bigquery.dataEditor role only, instead of roles/bigquery.dataOwner
options.C: Create a custom role by removing delete permissions, and add users to that role only
options.D: Create a custom role by removing delete permissions. Add users to the group, and then add the group to the custom role.

questions.explanation: When no predefined role perfectly matches the requirement (query access without delete), create a custom role. Google recommends assigning roles to groups rather than individual users. Option D follows both practices: custom role (removes bigquery.datasets.delete permission) + group-based assignment. Option C uses a custom role but assigns to individual users, not following the group best practice.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your company has a single sign-on (SSO) identity provider that supports SAML integration with service providers. Your company has users in Cloud Identity. You would like users to authenticate using your company's SSO provider. What should you do?

options.A: In Cloud Identity, set up SSO with Google as an identity provider to access custom SAML apps
options.B: In Cloud Identity, set up SSO with a third-party identity provider with Google as a service provider
options.C: Obtain OAuth 2.0 credentials, configure the user consent screen, and set up OAuth 2.0 for Mobile & Desktop Apps
options.D: Obtain OAuth 2.0 credentials, configure the user consent screen, and set up OAuth 2.0 for Web Server Applications

questions.explanation: When you want users to authenticate via an external SSO provider (like Okta, ADFS, Azure AD), you configure Cloud Identity as the SAML service provider and your corporate SSO as the identity provider. This is called 'SSO with third-party IdP.' Option A has it backwards — it would make Google the IdP for other apps. OAuth 2.0 (C, D) is for application authorization, not enterprise SSO federation.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your organization has a dedicated person who creates and manages all service accounts for GCP projects. You need to assign this person the minimum role for projects. What should you do?

options.A: Add the user to roles/iam.roleAdmin role
options.B: Add the user to roles/iam.securityAdmin role
options.C: Add the user to roles/iam.serviceAccountUser role
options.D: Add the user to roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin role

questions.explanation: roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin grants full control over service accounts: create, delete, update, enable, disable, and manage keys. This is the minimum role needed for someone whose sole job is managing service accounts. roles/iam.roleAdmin (A) manages roles, not service accounts. roles/iam.serviceAccountUser (C) only allows acting as a service account, not managing them.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You are building an archival solution and have selected Cloud Storage. Your users need to access this archived data once a quarter for regulatory requirements. You want a cost-efficient option. Which storage option should you use?

options.A: Cold Storage
options.B: Nearline Storage
options.C: Regional Storage
options.D: Multi-Regional Storage

questions.explanation: Access once per quarter (4 times per year) maps to Nearline Storage, which is designed for data accessed less than once per month. Nearline has a 30-day minimum storage duration. Coldline (approximately option A) would be appropriate for less than once per quarter access. Regional (C) and Multi-Regional (D) are Standard class — too expensive for rarely accessed archive data.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: A team of data scientists infrequently needs a GKE cluster. They require GPUs for some long-running, non-restartable jobs. You want to minimize cost. What should you do?

options.A: Enable node auto-provisioning on the GKE cluster
options.B: Create a VerticalPodAutoscaler for those workloads
options.C: Create a node pool with preemptible VMs and GPUs attached to those VMs
options.D: Create a node pool of instances with GPUs, and enable autoscaling on this node pool with a minimum size of 1

questions.explanation: Creating a GPU node pool with autoscaling (min=0, max=N) ensures GPU nodes spin up when needed and scale back when idle, minimizing costs. The jobs are non-restartable, so preemptible VMs (C) are inappropriate — they can be terminated at any time, which would fail non-restartable jobs. Node auto-provisioning (A) automatically creates node pools but doesn't account for the non-restartable constraint.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your organization has user identities in Active Directory. You want Active Directory to be the source of truth for identities and have full control over Google accounts used by employees. What should you do?

options.A: Use Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS) to synchronize users into Cloud Identity
options.B: Use the Cloud Identity APIs and write a script to synchronize users to Cloud Identity
options.C: Export users from Active Directory as a CSV and import them to Cloud Identity via the Admin Console
options.D: Ask each employee to create a Google account using self signup. Require that each employee use their company email address and password.

questions.explanation: Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS) is a free tool from Google that synchronizes users, groups, and other data from Active Directory to Cloud Identity on a scheduled basis. It keeps Cloud Identity in sync with AD as the source of truth. Writing custom scripts (B) would require ongoing maintenance. CSV import (C) is a one-time operation, not continuous synchronization. Self-signup (D) gives the organization no control over accounts.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You have successfully created a development environment in a project for an application using Compute Engine and Cloud SQL. You need to create a production environment. The security team has forbidden network routes between these 2 environments and has asked you to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Create a new project, enable the Compute Engine and Cloud SQL APIs in that project, and replicate the setup you have in the development environment
options.B: Create a new production subnet in the existing VPC and a new production Cloud SQL instance in your existing project
options.C: Create a new project, modify your existing VPC to be a Shared VPC, share that VPC with your new project, and replicate the setup
options.D: Ask the security team to grant you the Project Editor role in an existing production project used by another division

questions.explanation: Separate projects provide complete network isolation by default — there are no routes between projects unless explicitly configured (Shared VPC or VPC Peering). Creating a new project for production satisfies the security team's requirement. Subnets in the same VPC (B) can communicate with each other. Shared VPC (C) would create a network route between projects, violating the requirement.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: Your management has asked an external auditor to review resources in a specific project. The organization has enabled Domain Restricted Sharing allowing only your Cloud Identity domain. You want the auditor to only view, not modify, the resources. What should you do?

options.A: Ask the auditor for their Google account, and give them the Viewer role on the project
options.B: Ask the auditor for their Google account, and give them the Security Reviewer role on the project
options.C: Create a temporary account for the auditor in Cloud Identity, and give that account the Viewer role on the project
options.D: Create a temporary account for the auditor in Cloud Identity, and give that account the Security Reviewer role on the project

questions.explanation: Domain Restricted Sharing allows IAM grants only to identities within your Cloud Identity domain. An external auditor has an external Google account — adding their external account would violate this policy. Creating a temporary Cloud Identity account for the auditor satisfies the constraint. Viewer (not Security Reviewer) provides read-only access to all project resources, which meets the 'view only' requirement.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a workload running on Compute Engine that is critical to your business. You want to ensure that the data on the boot disk is backed up regularly and can be restored quickly. You also want older backups to be cleaned automatically. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Create a Cloud Function to create an instance template
options.B: Create a snapshot schedule for the disk using the desired interval
options.C: Create a cron job to create a new disk from the disk using gcloud
options.D: Create a Cloud Task to create an image and export it to Cloud Storage

questions.explanation: Compute Engine disk snapshot schedules are a native GCP feature that automatically creates snapshots at a specified frequency and retains them for a configurable period (auto-deletes old ones). Snapshots are incremental after the first, cost-efficient, and can be used to create new disks for rapid restoration. This requires no custom code or additional services.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You need to assign a Cloud IAM role to an external auditor. The auditor needs to review your GCP Audit Logs and Data Access logs. What should you do?

options.A: Assign the auditor the IAM role roles/logging.privateLogViewer. Perform the export of logs to Cloud Storage.
options.B: Assign the auditor the IAM role roles/logging.privateLogViewer. Direct the auditor to also review the logs for changes to Cloud IAM policy.
options.C: Assign the auditor's IAM user to a custom role that has logging.privateLogEntries.list permission. Perform the export of logs to Cloud Storage.
options.D: Assign the auditor's IAM user to a custom role that has logging.privateLogEntries.list permission. Direct the auditor to also review the logs for changes to Cloud IAM policy.

questions.explanation: roles/logging.privateLogViewer grants access to all logs including Data Access logs (which require special permission — regular Viewer can only see Admin Activity). Directing the auditor to review IAM policy change logs fulfills the audit requirement. Exporting to Cloud Storage (A, C) adds complexity without benefit if the auditor can review logs directly in Cloud Logging.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You are managing several GCP projects and need access to all logs for the past 60 days. You want to quickly analyze log contents. You want to follow Google-recommended practices to obtain combined logs for all projects. What should you do?

options.A: Navigate to Cloud Logging and select resource.labels.project_id="*"
options.B: Create a Cloud Logging Export with a Sink destination to a BigQuery dataset. Configure the table expiration to 60 days.
options.C: Create a Cloud Logging Export with a Sink destination to Cloud Storage. Create a lifecycle rule to delete objects after 60 days.
options.D: Configure a Cloud Scheduler job to read from Cloud Logging and store logs in BigQuery. Configure the table expiration to 60 days.

questions.explanation: Exporting logs to BigQuery combines logs from multiple projects into a single dataset, allows SQL-based analysis, and supports custom retention periods. BigQuery is ideal for quickly analyzing large volumes of structured log data with ad-hoc queries. Cloud Storage (C) stores logs cheaply but doesn't support easy querying. Cloud Scheduler (D) adds unnecessary complexity — log sinks export in real time.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You need to reduce GCP service costs for a division of your company using the fewest possible steps. You need to turn off all configured services in an existing GCP project. What should you do?

options.A: Verify that you are assigned the Project Owner IAM role for this project. Locate the project in the GCP console, click Shut down and then enter the project ID.
options.B: Verify that you are assigned the Project Owner IAM role for this project. Switch to the project in the GCP console, locate the resources and delete them.
options.C: Verify that you are assigned the Organizational Administrator IAM role for this project. Locate the project in the GCP console, enter the project ID and then click Shut down.
options.D: Verify that you are assigned the Organizational Administrators IAM role for this project. Switch to the project in the GCP console, locate the resources and delete them.

questions.explanation: The Project Owner role is sufficient to shut down a project (you don't need Org Admin). The Shut down option in the GCP Console marks the project for deletion, which stops all services and billing immediately. This is the fewest steps — a single action vs. individually deleting each resource (B). Projects enter a 30-day recovery window before permanent deletion.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You are configuring service accounts for an application that spans multiple projects. VMs in the web-applications project need access to BigQuery datasets in crm-databases-proj. What should you do?

options.A: Give project owner for web-applications appropriate roles to crm-databases-proj
options.B: Give project owner role to crm-databases-proj and the web-applications project
options.C: Give project owner role to crm-databases-proj and bigquery.dataViewer role to web-applications
options.D: Give bigquery.dataViewer role to crm-databases-proj and appropriate roles to web-applications

questions.explanation: Service accounts from one project can be granted IAM roles in another project. The web-applications service account needs bigquery.dataViewer on the datasets in crm-databases-proj (granted in crm-databases-proj). Granting project owner (A, B, C) is excessive — this is a classic least privilege violation. The data access role should be granted where the data lives (crm-databases-proj).

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: An employee's access to GCP was not removed until 2 weeks after termination. You need to find out if this employee accessed any sensitive customer information after their termination. What should you do?

options.A: View System Event Logs in Cloud Logging. Search for the user's email as the principal.
options.B: View System Event Logs in Cloud Logging. Search for the service account associated with the user.
options.C: View Data Access audit logs in Cloud Logging. Search for the user's email as the principal.
options.D: View the Admin Activity log in Cloud Logging. Search for the service account associated with the user.

questions.explanation: Data Access audit logs record API calls that read resource data (like accessing sensitive customer records). You search by the user's email address as the principal to find exactly what resources they accessed. Admin Activity logs (D) record administrative changes (creating/deleting resources), not data reads. System Event Logs (A, B) record system-level events, not user-initiated data access.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You need to create a custom IAM role for use with a GCP service. All permissions in the role must be suitable for production use. This will be the first version of the custom role. You want to clearly share the status with your organization. What should you do?

options.A: Use permissions in your role that use the 'supported' support level for role permissions. Set the role stage to ALPHA while testing the role permissions.
options.B: Use permissions in your role that use the 'supported' support level for role permissions. Set the role stage to BETA while testing the role permissions.
options.C: Use permissions in your role that use the 'testing' support level for role permissions. Set the role stage to ALPHA while testing the role permissions.
options.D: Use permissions in your role that use the 'testing' support level for role permissions. Set the role stage to BETA while testing the role permissions.

questions.explanation: For production use, only 'supported' permission support level is appropriate — 'testing' permissions are experimental and can change without notice. ALPHA stage signals to your organization that this is a new, potentially changing role being tested. GA would mean it's fully stable and ready for production. BETA would suggest it's past initial testing. ALPHA is correct for the first version during testing.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: Your company has a large quantity of unstructured data in different file formats. You want to perform ETL transformations using Dataflow. You need to make the data accessible on Google Cloud so it can be processed. What should you do?

options.A: Upload the data to BigQuery using the bq command line tool
options.B: Upload the data to Cloud Storage using the gsutil command line tool
options.C: Upload the data into Cloud SQL using the import function in the console
options.D: Upload the data into Cloud Spanner using the import function in the console

questions.explanation: Dataflow natively reads from Cloud Storage as a source for ETL pipelines. Cloud Storage handles any file format (CSV, JSON, Avro, Parquet, etc.) and is the universal staging area for unstructured data in GCP. Uploading unstructured data directly to BigQuery (A) requires it to be in a supported format. Cloud SQL (C) and Spanner (D) are relational databases, not suitable for arbitrary unstructured file formats.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You need to manage multiple GCP projects in the fewest steps possible. You want to configure the Cloud SDK CLI so that you can easily manage multiple projects. What should you do?

options.A: Create a configuration for each project. Activate the appropriate configuration when you work with each project.
options.B: Create a configuration for each project. Use gcloud init to update the configuration values when you need to work with a non-default project.
options.C: Use the default configuration for one project. Activate the appropriate configuration when you work with each project.
options.D: Use the default configuration for one project. Use gcloud init to update the configuration values when you need to work with a non-default project.

questions.explanation: gcloud named configurations let you save all settings (project, account, region, zone) for each environment. Creating one configuration per project and using gcloud config configurations activate to switch between them is the most efficient approach. gcloud init (B, D) requires answering interactive prompts each time you switch, which is slower and more error-prone.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: Your Managed Instance Group raised an alert stating that new instance creation has failed. You need to maintain the number of running instances specified by the template. What should you do?

options.A: Create an instance template that contains valid syntax which will be used by the instance group. Delete any persistent disks with the same name as instance names.
options.B: Create an instance template that contains valid syntax. Verify that the instance name and persistent disk name values are not the same in the template.
options.C: Verify that the instance template being used contains valid syntax. Delete any persistent disks with the same name as instance names. Set disks.autoDelete property to true.
options.D: Delete the current instance template and replace it with a new instance template. Verify that the instance name and persistent disk name values are not the same in the template. Set disks.autoDelete property to true.

questions.explanation: The most common cause of MIG instance creation failure is template syntax errors or resource conflicts. Persistent disks with the same name as instances cause conflicts when the MIG tries to create replacement instances. Creating a valid template and removing conflicting disk names resolves the issue. The disks.autoDelete property (C, D) prevents this from recurring but isn't the immediate fix — getting a working template and clearing the conflict is.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: Your company is moving to Google Cloud. You have multiple development teams that use Cassandra environments as backend databases. They all need a development environment that is isolated from other Cassandra instances. You want to move to GCP quickly with minimal support effort. What should you do?

options.A: Build an instruction guide to install Cassandra on Google Cloud. Make the instruction guide accessible to your developers.
options.B: Advise your developers to go to Cloud Marketplace. Ask the developers to launch a Cassandra image for their development work.
options.C: Build a Cassandra Compute Engine instance and take a snapshot of it. Use the snapshot to create instances for your developers.
options.D: Build a Cassandra Compute Engine instance and take a snapshot of it. Upload the snapshot to Cloud Storage and make it accessible to your developers. Build instructions to create a Compute Engine instance from the snapshot.

questions.explanation: GCP Marketplace offers pre-configured, one-click Cassandra deployments. Each developer team can independently launch their own isolated Cassandra instance from the Marketplace without requiring IT support or manual installation. This is the fastest approach with minimal support effort. Writing guides (A) or building custom snapshots (C, D) require significant upfront effort and ongoing support.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You have a Compute Engine instance hosting a production application. You want to receive an email if the instance consumes more than 90% of its CPU resources for more than 15 minutes. You want to use Google services. What should you do?

options.A: Create a consumer Gmail account. Write a script that monitors CPU usage. When CPU exceeds the threshold, have the script send an email via smtp.gmail.com.
options.B: Create a Cloud Monitoring Workspace, associate your GCP project with it. Create an Alerting Policy with the threshold as a trigger condition. Configure your email address in the notification channel.
options.C: Create a Cloud Monitoring Workspace, associate your GCP project with it. Write a script that monitors CPU usage and sends it as a custom metric to Cloud Monitoring. Create an uptime check for the instance.
options.D: In Cloud Logging, create a logs-based metric to extract CPU usage using a regular expression. In Cloud Monitoring, create an Alerting Policy based on this metric.

questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring has native CPU utilization metrics for Compute Engine instances and supports alerting policies with threshold conditions and duration windows. You can create an alerting policy that fires when CPU > 90% for 15 minutes and sends email via a notification channel. This requires no custom scripts. Uptime checks (C) monitor availability, not CPU. A custom script (A) is not using Google-managed monitoring services.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You have an application that uses Cloud Spanner. Traffic is very predictable. You want to automatically scale up or down the number of Spanner nodes depending on traffic. What should you do?

options.A: Create a cron job that runs on a scheduled basis to review Cloud Monitoring metrics, and then resize the Spanner instance accordingly
options.B: Create a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy to send an alert to oncall SRE emails when Cloud Spanner CPU exceeds the threshold. SREs would scale resources accordingly.
options.C: Create a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy to send an alert to Google Cloud Support email when Cloud Spanner CPU exceeds your threshold. Google support would scale resources.
options.D: Create a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy to send an alert to a webhook when Cloud Spanner CPU is over or under your threshold. Create a Cloud Function that listens to HTTP and resizes Spanner resources accordingly.

questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner doesn't have built-in autoscaling (unlike GKE or MIGs). The recommended automated approach is: Cloud Monitoring alert → webhook → Cloud Function → resize Spanner via API. This creates a fully automated feedback loop. Manual SRE response (B) introduces human latency and alert fatigue. A cron job (A) is time-based, not load-based — inadequate for predictable but varying traffic.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: Your company publishes large files on an Apache web server running on a Compute Engine instance. Apache is not the only application in the project. You want to receive an email when egress network costs for the server exceed $100 for the current month as measured by Google Cloud. What should you do?

options.A: Set up a budget alert on the project with an amount of 100 dollars, a threshold of 100%, and notification type of email
options.B: Set up a budget alert on the billing account with an amount of 100 dollars, a threshold of 100%, and notification type of email
options.C: Export the billing data to BigQuery. Create a Cloud Function that uses BigQuery to sum the egress network costs of the exported billing data for the Apache web server for the current month and sends an email if over 100 dollars. Schedule using Cloud Scheduler.
options.D: Use the Cloud Logging Agent to export Apache web server logs to Cloud Logging. Create a Cloud Function that uses BigQuery to parse the HTTP response log data for the current month and sends an email if the size of all HTTP responses, multiplied by current Google Cloud egress prices, totals over 100 dollars.

questions.explanation: Budget alerts at project level (A) would alert on all project costs, not just Apache egress. Billing export to BigQuery preserves SKU-level detail, allowing you to filter for specific resource labels (tagged to the Apache VM) and specific SKU (egress network). A Cloud Function triggered by Cloud Scheduler queries this data and sends targeted email alerts. Option D relies on approximations, not actual billing data.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You have designed a solution on Google Cloud that uses multiple Google Cloud products. Your company has asked you to estimate the costs. You need to provide estimates for the monthly total cost. What should you do?

options.A: For each Google Cloud product in the solution, review the pricing details on the products pricing page. Use the pricing calculator to total the monthly costs for each Google Cloud product.
options.B: For each Google Cloud product in the solution, review the pricing details on the products pricing page. Create a Google Sheet that summarizes the expected monthly costs.
options.C: Provision the solution on Google Cloud. Leave it provisioned for 1 week. Navigate to the Billing Report page. Multiply the 1-week cost to determine monthly costs.
options.D: Provision the solution on Google Cloud. Leave it provisioned for 1 week. Use Cloud Monitoring to determine the provisioned and used resource amounts. Multiply the 1-week cost to determine monthly costs.

questions.explanation: The GCP Pricing Calculator is the official tool for estimating costs before provisioning. You enter each service's expected usage and it calculates monthly cost estimates. This is the correct approach for pre-deployment cost estimation. Provisioning the solution for a week (C, D) to estimate costs is expensive and unnecessary — the pricing calculator is designed exactly for this purpose.

questions.section: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
questions.text: You have an application that receives SSL-encrypted TCP traffic on port 443. Clients are located all over the world. You want to minimize latency for the clients. Which load balancing option should you use?

options.A: HTTPS Load Balancer
options.B: Network Load Balancer
options.C: SSL Proxy Load Balancer
options.D: Internal TCP/UDP Load Balancer with a firewall rule allowing ingress traffic from 0.0.0.0/0

questions.explanation: The SSL Proxy Load Balancer is a global, Layer 4 load balancer that terminates SSL connections and can handle non-HTTP SSL traffic (like LDAPS, PostgreSQL over SSL). It has global anycast IP, so clients connect to the nearest Google edge PoP, minimizing latency. HTTPS LB (A) is for HTTP/HTTPS traffic specifically and terminates SSL too, but the SSL Proxy is appropriate for non-HTTP SSL on port 443. Internal LB (D) is not accessible to global clients.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: You have an application on a Compute Engine instance experiencing excessive disk read throttling on its Zonal SSD Persistent Disk (350 GB). The application primarily reads large files from disk. You want to maximize throughput while minimizing costs. What should you do?

options.A: Increase the size of the disk to 1 TB
options.B: Increase the allocated CPU to the instance
options.C: Migrate to use a Local SSD on the instance
options.D: Migrate to use a Regional SSD on the instance

questions.explanation: Local SSDs are physically attached to the server hosting the VM, providing much higher IOPS and throughput than Persistent Disks (both Zonal and Regional). For large file sequential reads, Local SSD provides up to 2.4 TB/s read throughput vs. much lower limits on Persistent Disk. Local SSD is also cheaper per GB than Regional SSD (D). Increasing disk size (A) increases throughput limits but not as dramatically. CPU (B) is not the bottleneck here.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: Your Dataproc cluster runs in a single VPC subnet with range 172.16.20.128/25. There are no private IP addresses available in the VPC network. You want to add new VMs to communicate with your cluster using the minimum number of steps. What should you do?

options.A: Modify the existing subnet range to 172.16.20.0/24
options.B: Create a new Secondary IP Range in the VPC and configure the VMs to use that range
options.C: Create a new VPC network for the VMs. Enable VPC Peering between the VMs' VPC network and the Dataproc cluster VPC network.
options.D: Create a new VPC network for the VMs with a subnet of 172.32.0.0/16. Enable VPC network Peering between the Dataproc VPC and the VMs VPC. Configure custom Route exchange.

questions.explanation: You can expand an existing GCP subnet range by changing the prefix length to a wider mask (e.g., /25 to /24 doubles the available IPs). 172.16.20.0/24 includes 172.16.20.128/25 as a subset, so existing IPs remain valid. This is a non-destructive in-place operation with one step. Creating new VPCs with peering (C, D) requires multiple steps and adds routing complexity.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: You manage an App Engine Service that aggregates and visualizes data from BigQuery in a different project managed by another team. You want your application to be able to read data from the BigQuery dataset. What should you do?

options.A: Ask the other team to grant your default App Engine Service account the role of BigQuery Job User
options.B: Ask the other team to grant your default App Engine Service account the role of BigQuery Data Viewer
options.C: In Cloud IAM of your project, ensure that the default App Engine service account has the role of BigQuery Data Viewer
options.D: In Cloud IAM of your project, grant a newly created service account from the other team the role of BigQuery Job User in your project

questions.explanation: The App Engine service account needs BigQuery Data Viewer in the project where the data lives (the other team's project). Only the other team can grant IAM roles in their project. BigQuery Data Viewer grants the ability to list and read data in datasets and tables. BigQuery Job User (A) allows running jobs but doesn't grant access to the data itself. Granting roles in your own project (C) doesn't affect access to another project's data.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You need to create a copy of a custom Compute Engine virtual machine to facilitate an expected increase in application traffic. What should you do?

options.A: Create a Compute Engine snapshot of your base VM. Create your images from that snapshot.
options.B: Create a Compute Engine snapshot of your base VM. Create your instances from that snapshot.
options.C: Create a custom Compute Engine image from a snapshot. Create your images from that image.
options.D: Create a custom Compute Engine image from a snapshot. Create your instances from that image.

questions.explanation: The correct workflow for creating multiple identical VMs from a custom VM: snapshot the disk → create a custom image from the snapshot → launch instances from the custom image. A custom image provides a stable, reusable base that can be used in instance templates and MIGs. Creating instances directly from a snapshot (B) is possible but creating a custom image first is the recommended best practice for reusability and use in instance templates.

questions.section: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
questions.text: An application deployed on a single Compute Engine instance writes logs to disk. Users start reporting errors with the application. You want to diagnose the problem. What should you do?

options.A: Navigate to Cloud Logging and view the application logs
options.B: Connect to the instance's serial console and read the application logs
options.C: Configure a Health Check on the instance and set a Low Healthy Threshold value
options.D: Install and configure the Cloud Logging Agent and view the logs from Cloud Logging

questions.explanation: Application logs written to disk on a Compute Engine instance are not automatically exported to Cloud Logging. The Cloud Logging Agent (formerly Stackdriver Logging Agent) must be installed and configured to collect logs from disk files and send them to Cloud Logging. Option A would only work if the agent were already installed and configured. The serial console (B) shows boot logs, not application logs.

questions.section: Configuring access and security
questions.text: An application generates daily reports in a VM in project corp-iot-insights. Your team operates only in the project corp-aggregate-reports and needs a copy of the daily exports in the bucket corp-aggregate-reports-storage. You want to configure access using as few steps as possible while following Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.A: Move both projects under the same folder
options.B: Grant the VM Service Account the role Storage Object Creator on corp-aggregate-reports-storage
options.C: Create a Shared VPC network between both projects. Grant the VM Service Account the role Storage Object Creator on corp-iot-insights.
options.D: Make corp-aggregate-reports-storage public and create a folder with a pseudo-randomized suffix name. Share the folder with the IoT team.

questions.explanation: The VM's service account can be granted IAM roles on buckets in other projects. Granting Storage Object Creator on the destination bucket allows the VM to write files there directly. This is one step and follows least privilege (only create, not read or delete). Moving projects under the same folder (A) doesn't automatically grant cross-project bucket access. Making the bucket public (D) is a security anti-pattern.

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: You built an application on your development laptop that uses Application Default Credentials for authentication. You want to migrate this application to a Compute Engine VM using Google-recommended practices and minimal changes. What should you do?

options.A: Assign appropriate access for Google services to the service account used by the Compute Engine VM
options.B: Create a service account with appropriate access for Google services, and configure the application to use this account
options.C: Store credentials for service accounts with appropriate access for Google services in a config file, and deploy this config file with your application
options.D: Store credentials for your user account with appropriate access for Google services in a config file, and deploy this config file with your application

questions.explanation: Application Default Credentials (ADC) on Compute Engine automatically uses the VM's attached service account — no code changes needed. You just ensure the service account has the right permissions. This requires no credential files and no code changes. Storing credentials in config files (C, D) is an anti-pattern — it creates credential management burden and security risks. Option B requires code changes to explicitly specify the service account.

questions.section: Setting up a cloud solution environment
questions.text: You need to create a Compute Engine instance in a new project that doesn't exist yet. What should you do?

options.A: Using the Cloud SDK, create a new project, enable the Compute Engine API in that project, and then create the instance specifying your new project
options.B: Enable the Compute Engine API in the Cloud Console, use the Cloud SDK to create the instance, and then use the --project flag to specify a new project
options.C: Using the Cloud SDK, create the new instance, and use the --project flag to specify the new project. Answer yes when prompted by Cloud SDK to enable the Compute Engine API.
options.D: Enable the Compute Engine API in the Cloud Console. Go to the Compute Engine section to create a new instance, and look for the 'Create In A New Project' option.

questions.explanation: The sequence for creating a VM in a new project: (1) gcloud projects create <PROJECT_ID>, (2) gcloud services enable compute.googleapis.com --project <PROJECT_ID> to enable the Compute Engine API, (3) gcloud compute instances create with --project flag. Creating a project and enabling APIs are prerequisites. The Cloud SDK doesn't prompt to enable APIs automatically when using --project to specify a non-existent project (C).

questions.section: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
questions.text: Your company runs a batch process in an on-premises server that takes around 30 hours to complete. The task runs monthly, can be performed offline, and must be restarted if interrupted. You want to migrate this workload to the cloud while minimizing cost. What should you do?

options.A: Migrate the workload to a Compute Engine Preemptible VM
options.B: Migrate the workload to a GKE cluster with Preemptible nodes
options.C: Migrate the workload to a Compute Engine VM. Start and stop the instance as needed.
options.D: Create an Instance Template with Preemptible VMs. Create a Managed Instance Group from the template and adjust Target CPU Utilization.

questions.explanation: The job takes 30 hours, but Spot/Preemptible VMs have a maximum runtime of 24 hours — they are automatically stopped after 24 hours. Since the job must complete in one run and Preemptible VMs can't run for 30 hours, option A and B are invalid. The correct approach is an on-demand VM that runs for 30 hours and is stopped when done. This minimizes cost compared to always-on VMs while meeting the runtime requirement.
